item.title: Best practices for rapidly deploying Landing Zone Accelerator on AWS
item.author: Lei Shi
item.content:encoded: Landing Zone Accelerator on AWS (LZA) enables customers to deploy a flexible, configuration-driven solution to establish a landing zone while also leveraging AWS Control Tower. At AWS Professional Services, we’ve helped customers deploy and configure LZA hundreds of times. A common request we encounter is integrating LZA configuration into customers’ existing GitOps workflows. GitOps has emerged as a leading model for Infrastructure as Code (IaC), helping organizations automate and manage their cloud infrastructure. The model uses Git repositories as the single source of truth for infrastructure configuration, enabling teams to maintain consistent, version-controlled environments.In this blog, we will focus on common LZA implementation steps based on our experience, helping customers jump-start their LZA environment and implement GitOps for their AWS infrastructure management. First, we will demonstrate how to leverage LZA while complying with your organization’s policies such as private package repositories. Next, we will guide you through a new installation of LZA that takes advantage of an auto-generated starter set of configuration files. Finally, we will direct you to another blog post that will enable you to leverage GitOps for ongoing management of your LZA configuration.The LZA solution leverages two distinct repositories; one for the LZA source code, and another for your organization’s specific configuration files. LZA creates two separate AWS CodePipelines , which are used to install the LZA solution and apply your organization’s specific configuration. Figure 1 illustrates the association between repositories and pipelines. By default, when installing LZA, the solution uses GitHub as the source and pulls the installation files published by AWS from the official LZA GitHub repository.Figure 1. Landing Zone Accelerator solution componentsDeploy LZA as a new installStep 1: Preparing your enterprise private GitHub to host LZA source code.Customers may choose to deploy LZA from the official AWS GitHub repository for LZA, but we often we find customers have policies in place that require these types of packages to be deployed from a private repository managed by the organization. For customers using GitHub privately in their enterprise, this can be as easy as cloning the LZA source code repository into your own private GitHub repository, enabling you to take advantage of policies and controls within your organization. Before moving to the next step, take a moment and clone the repository into your own private repository. A GitHub personal access token stored in AWS Secrets Manager is required to enable the stack to access your private repository. Before deploying LZA, follow these instructions to enable stack access to your repository.Step 2: In the organization management account, install LZA as a CloudFormation Stack.To get started, we will be going through a new installation of the LZA solution. The following steps provide specific parameter options to the CloudFormation template to support a new installation of LZA.Specify the following parameters for Source Code Repository Configuration, see Figure 2.For , specify a name you like.For , choose .For , specify your GitHub account owner ID.For , specify your cloned LZA source code repositoryFor , specify the branch name of your LZA source code repository.Figure 2. LZA installer stack parameters – source code repositorySpecify the following parameters for Configuration Repository Configuration, see Figure 3.For Configuration Repository Location, choose .For Use Existing Config Repository, choose .For Existing Config Repository Name, Existing Config Repository Branch Name, Existing Config Repository Owner, and Existing Config Repository CodeConnection ARN, leave blank.We intentionally want to use S3 for the configuration repository because as the LZA solution is installed, it will auto-generate a set of starter configuration YAML files and deploy them for us in S3. This makes it very easy to get started with an initial set of customized YAML files for your environment. We choose “No” in the Use Existing Config Repository field, to have LZA to perform a new LZA installation.Figure 3. LZA installer stack parameters – configuration repositoryChoose and complete the remainder of the stack settings.Finally, choose to launch the CloudFormation stack.The installer stack typically takes minutes to complete (See Figure 4).Figure 4. LZA installer stack completionStep 3: Validate two LZA pipelines are created and successfully completed in AWS CodePipeline console.After the CloudFormation stack completes, open the AWS CodePipeline console. You’ll see a new pipeline named “AWSAccelerator-Installer” running (See Figure 5). This is the LZA Installer pipeline, and it’s connected to the GitHub source repository you specified in Step 2 above with parameters from 2 to 5. This Installer pipeline automatically generates a set of LZA configuration files stored as a compressed ZIP archive in Amazon S3. It will be designated as configuration repository of the LZA solution.Figure 5. AWSAccelerator-Installer pipeline creationWhen the AWSAccelerator-Installer pipeline completes, the solution automatically creates and runs a second pipeline named “AWSAccelerator-Pipeline” as shown in Figure 6. This pipeline connects to both the GitHub source repository, and the newly created configuration repository in Amazon S3. The AWSAccelerator-Pipeline is the pipeline that manages your landing zone deployment and customization.Figure 6. AWSAccelerator-Pipeline created from the AWSAccelerator-Installer pipelineAfter the AWSAccelerator-Pipeline completes, your LZA solution is ready for customization.Step 4: Migrate the LZA configuration repository from S3 to GitHub With the AWSAccelerator-Pipeline completed, your initial landing zone is now deployed, leveraging the configuration stored in your S3 bucket. For some customers, they may need to ensure that changes to the landing zone configuration are controlled through their existing GitOps processes and tooling. See Figure 7 as an example where the S3 configuration files have been copied to a customer owned GitHub repository. This transition step can be performed in future LZA upgrade window when there is a new release of LZA source code, or right after the initial LZA installation completes in Step 3For more information on migrating from S3 to GitHub, follow this guide to configure your AWSAccelerator-Pipelines with AWS CodeConnection.Figure 7. CodeConnection based LZA Configuration RepositoryIn this post, we explored key steps to streamline your LZA implementation journey. By demonstrating how to work with your private package repositories, providing guidance on leveraging auto-generated configuration files, and introducing GitOps-based management, we’ve outlined a practical path to establish and maintain a robust AWS infrastructure foundation. These approaches can significantly reduce the time and complexity typically associated with LZA deployments while ensuring compliance with organizational policies. We encourage you to try these implementation steps and explore the referenced resources to enhance your AWS cloud operations. For more information about Landing Zone Accelerator, visit the AWS Landing Zone Accelerator on GitHub.

item.title: Digest #162: IBM Acquires HashiCorp, No TCP/UDP, GitHub Data Theft, FinOps Future, Postgres Graph, AWS Config, Airflow, Kubernetes, Nginx, Terraform & More!
item.author: Mohamed Labouardy
item.content:encoded: Welcome to this week’s edition of the DevOps Bulletin!IBM just completed its acquisition of HashiCorp, and we ask: what would happen if we stopped using TCP and UDP? Learn how thousands of GitHub mods and cracks might be stealing your data, see what happened in a SQL injection court, and explore a world where logs rule—with CPU usage up by 3,200%.Our featured podcast looks at the future of FinOps, giving you a fresh look at managing cloud costs for tomorrow.In our tutorials, find easy guides on using Postgres as a graph database, automating cost checks with AWS Config, and cutting an AWS Glue bill from $10,000 to $400 using Airflow. Learn about Kubernetes audit log gotchas, slicing up SLOs, advanced Nginx hardening, Amazon EKS auto mode with Terraform, GitHub Copilot for Azure DevOps, and mastering infrastructure provisioning.We also spotlight cool open-source projects:• – a simple desktop API client• – a web app for exploring log data• – a mini database written in Go• – a lightweight tool for PostgreSQL CDC• – for smoother Terraform Cloud workflows• – a toolkit for code checking• – a Python tool written in RustAll this and more in this week’s DevOps Bulletin—don’t miss out! is a desktop API client that interacts with REST, GraphQL, Server Sent Events (SSE), WebSocket, and gRPC APIs. is a web application designed to provide an intuitive interface for exploring log data. is a mini relational database that is fully written in Go. is a lightweight and modular CDC (Change Data Capture) utility for PostgreSQL. allows Terraform Cloud users to get apply-before-merge workflows in their Pull Requests. is a fast, feature-rich, and open-source static analysis toolkit for writing and running code checkers. is a Python tool to enforce dependencies and interfaces, written in Rust.If you have feedback to share or are interested in sponsoring this newsletter, feel free to reach out via , or simply reply to this email.

item.title: Docker Engine v28: Hardening Container Networking by Default
item.author: Tim Welsh
item.content:encoded: Docker simplifies containerization by removing runtime complexity and making app development seamless. With Docker Engine v28, we’re taking another step forward in security by ensuring containers aren’t unintentionally accessible from local networks. This update isn’t about fixing a single vulnerability — it’s about security hardening so your containers stay safe. When you run a container on the default Docker “bridge” network, Docker sets up NAT (Network Address Translation) rules using your system’s firewall (via iptables). For example, the following command forwards traffic from port 8080 on your host to port 80 in the container. docker run -d -p 8080:80 my-web-appHowever, if your host’s chain is permissive (i.e., by default) is enabled, unpublished ports could also be remotely accessible under certain conditions.This only affects hosts on the same physical/link-layer network as the Docker host. In multi-tenant LAN environments or other shared local networks, someone connected on an RFC1918 subnet (such as or ) could reach unpublished container ports if they knew (or guessed) its IP address.This behavior only affects Linux users running Docker versions earlier than 28.0.0 with . Docker Desktop is affected.If you installed Docker on a single machine and used our defaults, without manually customizing your firewall settings, you’re likely unaffected by upgrading. However, you could be impacted if:You deliberately set your host’s chain to and rely on accessing containers by their IP from other machines on the LAN.You bound containers to or another loopback interface but still route external traffic to them using advanced host networking tricks.You require direct container access from subnets, VLANs, or other broadcast domains explicitly publishing ports (i.e., no flags).If any of these exceptions apply to you, you might notice that containers previously reachable by direct IP now appear blocked unless you opt out or reconfigure your networking.This exposure would’ve required being on the same local network or otherwise having route-level access to the container’s RFC1918 IP range. It affect machines across the public internet. However, a malicious user could discover unpublished container ports, within LAN settings or corporate environments, and connect to them. For instance, they might add a custom route to the container’s subnet, using the following command:ip route add 172.17.0.0/16 via 192.168.0.10From there, if is your Docker host with a permissive firewall, the attacker could send packets straight to (the container’s IP).If you’ve been impacted, we recommend taking the following three steps:1. Upgrade to Docker Engine 28.0Docker Engine 28.0 now drops traffic to unpublished ports by default. This “secure by default” approach prevents containers from being unexpectedly accessible on the LAN. Most users won’t notice a difference, published ports will continue to function as usual (), and unpublished ports remain private as intended.2. Decide if you need the old behavior (opt-out)If you connect to containers over the LAN without publishing ports, you have a couple of options:Option 1: Disable Docker’s policy In , add the following:{ "ip-forward-no-drop": true } Or you can run Docker with the flag. This preserves a globally open chain. But keep in mind that Docker still drops traffic for unpublished ports using separate rules. See Option 2 for a fully unprotected alternative.Option 2: Create a “nat-unprotected” networkdocker network create -d bridge \\ -o com.docker.network.bridge.gateway_mode_ipv4=nat-unprotected \\ my_unprotected_net 3. Consider custom iptables managementAdvanced users with complex network setups can manually configure iptables to allow exactly the traffic they need. But this route is only recommended if you’re comfortable managing firewall rules.In previous Docker Engine versions, Docker’s reliance on a permissive chain meant that containers on the default bridge network could be reached if: was enabled (often auto-enabled by Docker if it wasn’t already).The system-wide chain was set to .Another machine on the same LAN (or an attached subnet) routed traffic to the container’s IP address.In Docker 28.0, we now explicitly drop unsolicited inbound traffic to each container’s internal IP unless that port was explicitly published ( or ). This doesn’t affect local connections from the Docker host itself, but it does block remote LAN connections to unpublished ports.Attacks: a real-world exampleSuppose you have two hosts on the same RFC1918 subnet:DockerHost runs a container with IP , and Docker’s firewall policy is effectively “ACCEPT.” In this situation, an attacker could run the following command:ip route add 172.17.0.2/32 via 10.0.0.3 nc 172.17.0.2 3306If MySQL was listening on port 3306 in the container (unpublished), Docker might still forward that traffic. The container sees a connection from , and no authentication is enforced by Docker’s network stack alone. Mitigations in Docker Engine 28.0By enforcing a drop rule for unpublished ports, Docker 28.0 now prevents the above scenario by default.1. Default drop for unpublished portsEnsures that local traffic to container IPs is discarded unless explicitly published.2. Targeted adjustments to policyOriginally, if Docker had to enable IP forwarding, it would set by default. Now, Docker only applies that if necessary and extends the same logic to IPv6. You can opt-out with or in the config file.3. Unpublished ports stay privateThe container remains accessible from the host itself, but not from other devices on the LAN (unless you intentionally choose “nat-unprotected”).Why we’re making this change nowSome users have raised concerns about local-network exposure for years (see issues like #14041 and #22054). But there’s been a lot of change since then:: Use cases often revolved around single-node setups where any extra exposure was mitigated by typical dev/test workflows.: Overlay networks, multi-host orchestration, and advanced routing became mainstream. Suddenly, scenarios once relegated to specialized setups became common.: Docker now underpins critical workloads in complex environments. Everyone benefits from safer defaults, even if it means adjusting older assumptions.By introducing these changes in Docker Engine 28.0, we align with today’s best practices: don’t expose anything without explicit user intent. It’s a shift away from relying on users to configure their own firewalls if they want to lock things down.Backward compatibility and the path forwardThese changes are backward compatible for users who rely on direct container IP access from a local LAN. For the majority, the new defaults lock down containers more securely without breaking typical scenarios.Still, we strongly advise upgrading to 28.0.1 or later so you can benefit from:: Unpublished ports remain private unless you publish or explicitly opt out.: Published vs. unpublished ports are now unambiguously enforced by iptables rules.: Users can adopt the new defaults without becoming iptables experts.Before you upgrade, here’s a recommended checklist to run through to make sure you’re set up for success with the latest release:Examine current firewall settingsRun and . If you see in the chain, and you depend on that for multi-subnet or direct container access, plan accordingly.Test in a controlled environmentSpin up Docker Engine 28.0 on a staging environment. Attempt to reach containers that you previously accessed directly. Then, verify which connections still work and which are now blocked.If you do need the old behavior, set "ip-forward-no-drop": true or use a “nat-unprotected” network. Otherwise, enjoy the heightened security defaults.Watch for unexpected connection errors or service downtime. If something breaks, check if it’s caused by Docker’s new drop rules before rolling back any changes.By hardening default networking rules in Docker Engine 28.0, we’re reducing accidental container exposure on local networks. Most users can continue without disruption and can just enjoy the extra peace of mind. But if you rely on older behaviors, you can opt out or create specialized networks that bypass these rules.We appreciate the feedback from the community and encourage you to reach out if you have questions or run into surprises after upgrading. You can find us on GitHub issues, our community forums, or your usual support channels.

item.title: One of the many features that nestJs offers is its native support of microservices architecture.
item.author: Mouloud hasrane
item.content:encoded: Building Scalable Microservices with NestJSOne of the many features that nestJs offers is its native support of microservices architecture. When using microservices, you can use all the features that nests have in a normal monolithic architecture, like decorators,guards, pipes, etc. But before we jump into code implementation, let’s go back and talk about what microservices are and some core concepts you need to understand.when dealing with an application programming interface (API) we usually send a request to one server that handles all kinds of requests that our application might need, it handles all of the logic inside on app or server and sends the appropriate response On the other hand Micro Services pattern is quite different instead of having one app or server handling all of the requests we have multiple servers called , each one of them is responsible for handling some part of our app logic, but you may ask why would we want to use this instead of our traditional architecture and avoid all of the overhead of setting it up,w ellSeparation of concerns and decoupling:w ith this setup, every service handles some aspect of our application so it is easier to scale and locate errors if they exist: because each service exists as an independent node if it goes down it only affects the logic aspect in handles, not the whole serverscalability and performance: when we separate services into different nodes each one of them will have less job to do because it only focuses on some aspect of the app, not the whole app, with each node existing as a standalone app we can easily scale the services that are used frequentlyNow after that, you might Ask : Now, you might ask: how do these services communicate with each other?Ande we need to introduce the concept of a transport layer, you can imagine it for now as the route or the bridge that links all of the services together it sends data across multiple services :fmo us transport layers are :What is cool about nests that is allows you to use your preferred transport layer from the multiple ones it offers and you can easily change it in the configuration without changing the code later (say you are using Tcp and after a while, you wanted to switch over to a message queue like RMQ you just have to change it in the configuration of the module without changing anything in the code)Before we jump into coding (I know it took too long but this is not your average code tutorial blog we need to understand what exactly each piece of code does when we write it) we need to discuss one last thing that is the types of microservices, in this context ,we are focusing on types regarding how are req-res are handled and we will focus on 2 types:Request-response (Message pattern):Here we send a message( which is just a piece of data) over to the right service over a transport layer, and then wait for it to return a response to useHere we are also sending a message or in this case, it is called an event but we don’t actually expect or wait for a response instead side effects are just happening in the appropriate service(s) In most cases events are unidirectional meaning that the data only goes in one waynow that we have a basic understanding of what micro services architecture is all about let’s dive into how to implement it using nestJsExample of A micro Services Systemhere is what a micro Services system look likeWe will try to build a simple e-commerce example to showcase microServices in nestsFirst we Create a normal nests application this application is often called the producer because unlike other nodes it is an HTTP app that is responsible for getting the requests and sending them to the appropriate servicethen we need to install the microServices module$ npm i --save @nestjs/microservices3 . Install the transport layer here I’m using Rabbit mq feel free to use anyone you like (check the documentation for supported Transport layers)$ npm i --save amqplib amqp-connection-managerWe define a simple dto for validationimport { IsEmail, IsNumber, IsString } from 'class-validator';export class orderDto { @IsString() @IsEmail() @IsNumber()}Then we register our services inside the app Module or the feature module if you want better separation, using the microservices module that we just downloaded here we will only use the payment and orders serviceimport { OrdersController } from './orders.controller';import { ClientsModule, Transport } from '@nestjs/microservices'; @Module({ imports: [ { transport: Transport.RMQ, URLs: ['amqp://localhost:5672'], queueOptions: { durable: true, // Make the queue persistent }, }, ]), { transport: Transport.RMQ, URLs: ['amqp://localhost:5672'], queueOptions: { durable: true, // Make the queue persistent }, }, ]), ], controllers: [OrdersController], providers: [OrdersService],})export class OrdersModule {}Here I’m using RabbitMq which is a powerful message queue : is a transport layer that uses the FIFO Data structure and holds the messages in a queue until the first message gets processed by the consumer (service), what is cool about it is that if the consumer is down it will wait until it is up again (unlike tcp for example which if the service is down the message will just be lost)Here is a simple controller that handles 2 requests/* eslint-disable prettier/prettier */import { Body, Controller, Post } from '@nestjs/common';import { OrdersService } from './orders.service';import { orderDto } from './orderDto';@Controller('orders')export class OrdersController { constructor(private read-only ordersService: OrdersService) { } OrderAdded(@Body() order:orderDto){ return this.ordersService.handleOrder(order); } payOrder(@Body() order:orderDto){ return this.ordersService.handle payment(order); }/* eslint-disable prettier/prettier */import { Inject, Injectable } from '@nestjs/common';import { orderDto } from './orderDto';import { ClientProxy } from '@nestjs/microservices';@Injectable()export class OrdersService { constructor(@Inject("ORDER_SERVICE") private RSERVICE:ClientProxy,@Inject('PAYMENT_SERVICE') private PAYMENTQueue:ClientProxy ){} handleOrder(order :orderDto){ this.RSERVICE.emit('ORDER_PLACED',order); return {message:"ORDER ADDED!"} handle payment(order: orderDto) { this.PAYMENTQueue.emit('PAYMENT',order); return { message: 'PAYMENT DONE!', }Here is where the magic happens first we Inject services registered earlier in the module and then we emitting events the first String in the emit method is the event pattern which is how the service now what logic to execute and the second param is the payload which is the actual data that the service needs to work with. here we are using .emit because we are using event-based architecture if you want to use a message you just use .send insteadAnd then we need to create another nest application which is the service itself here is how to define the main of the appimport { NestFactory } from '@nestjs/core';import { AppModule } from './app.module';import { Transport } from '@nestjs/microservices';async function bootstrap() { const app = await NestFactory.createMicroservice(AppModule, { transport: Transport.RMQ, options: { URLs: ['amqp://localhost:5672'], queue: 'order_queue', durable: true, }, await app.listen();bootstrap();what changes that we create a microService instead of a nest application and then we define the appropriate transport layer that this service uses to communicate with the producer and here is to define the controller to handle upcoming eventsimport { Controller } from '@nestjs/common';import { OrdersService } from './orders.service';import { Ctx, EventPattern, Payload, RmqContext } from '@nestjs/microservices';import { OrderDto } from './orderDto';@Controller('orders')export class OrdersController { constructor(private readonly ordersService: OrdersService) {} @EventPattern('ORDER_PLACED') handleOrders(@Payload() order: OrderDto, @Ctx() context: RmqContext) { return this.ordersService.handleOrder(order);}note that the Event Pattern is the same one we sent Earlier here we get Acces to the payload that we send and the Excution context which contains some metadata that are out of the scope of this article what cool now is that we can register another Service inside this service and send events to it as well like we’re doing here in the serviceimport { Inject, Injectable } from '@nestjs/common';import { ClientProxy } from '@nestjs/microservices';import { OrderDto } from './orderDto';@Injectable()export class OrdersService { @Inject('PAYMENT_SERVICE') private readonly paymentClient: ClientProxy, handleOrder(order: OrderDto) { console.log('PROCESSING ORDER', order); console.log('SENDING ORDER TO PAYMENT SERVICE'); this.paymentClient.emit('PAYMENT', order); }and Payment Client is another service built just like this oneIn this article, we explored how to build a microservices architecture using NestJS and RabbitMQ. We discussed the benefits of microservices, such as scalability, fault tolerance, and separation of concerns, and demonstrated how NestJS simplifies inter-service communication.By leveraging RabbitMQ as a message broker, we ensured reliable, asynchronous communication between services, making our system more resilient and efficient. The flexibility of NestJS also allows us to switch transport layers easily, giving us the freedom to adapt to different use cases.As you continue building microservices, consider factors like service discovery, monitoring, and security to enhance your system further. Experiment with different transport layers, and explore tools like Kubernetes for orchestration. Microservices can be complex, but with the right tools and architecture, they can unlock powerful scalability and maintainability for your applications. 🚀

item.title: Understanding Replication and Sharding in System Design
item.author: Mouloud hasrane
item.content:encoded: When discussing system design, terms like sharding, partitioning, replication, and master-slave relationships frequently come up. If these concepts seem confusing, don’t worry! This article will clarify the differences between replication and sharding to help you build a solid foundation in distributed database systems. ps: images are from building data intensive systems book be sure to check it outReplication involves creating multiple instances of a database that contain the same data as the main instance. This allows requests to be distributed among replicas, improving system performance and availability. But how do we decide which requests go to which instance? Replication can be categorized into different types, but in practice, two are most commonly used:1. Master-Slave Replication (Leader-Follower Replication)In this model, a leader (master) database handles both read and write operations, while followers (slaves) handle only read operations. Any write operation made to the leader is replicated to the followers, either synchronously or asynchronously (we’ll discuss these approaches in detail in a future article).Most systems are read-heavy rather than write-heavy, meaning they perform far more read operations than writes. By distributing read queries to multiple followers, we can significantly reduce the load on the leader instance, improving performance and scalability.2. Master-Master Replication (Leader-Leader Replication)Now, imagine our system has grown to the point where we need multiple data centers worldwide to minimize latency. If we rely on leader-follower replication, choosing a single leader across all data centers would lead to slow write operations for users far from that leader.With leader-leader replication, each data center has its own leader. When a write operation is performed at one leader, it is propagated to all other leaders, which then replicate it to their followers. Challenges:While this approach improves performance and fault tolerance, it introduces challenges such as write conflicts, where two leaders handle conflicting writes at the same time. Handling such conflicts requires careful resolution strategies, such as conflict-free replicated data types (CRDTs) or last-write-wins (LWW) mechanisms.Unlike replication, which creates copies of the same database, sharding (also called partitioning) splits the database into smaller subsets, called shards, each containing a portion of the data. This allows for better scalability by distributing requests across multiple shards. How do we determine which shard a request should go to?Two common techniques for sharding are:In this approach, data is divided into shards based on a predefined range of values. Think of it like an encyclopedia where different volumes contain different alphabetic sections of words.Example: User IDs from 1–1000 go to Shard A, 1001–2000 go to Shard B, and so on.Advantage: Simple to implement.Disadvantage: Can lead to hot spots — if a specific range receives more requests than others, that shard will become overloaded.Here, a hash function is applied to a data attribute (e.g., User ID) to determine which shard should store the data. A well-designed hash function distributes data evenly across shards.say for example If hash(UserID) % 3 == 0, the request is sent to Shard A; if hash(UserID) % 3 == 1, it goes to Shard B, and so on.Advantage: Ensures even distribution and prevents hot spots.Disadvantage: Can make it difficult to perform range queries, as data is spread across multiple shards unpredictably.As data grows and system demands change, sharded databases must be rebalanced to maintain efficiency. This involves redistributing data and queries among nodes while minimizing disruptions. Rebalancing is crucial to:Prevent overload on specific shards.Maintain system availability for read/write operations.Minimize data movement to reduce network and disk I/O overhead.We will discuss different rebalancing strategies and common pitfalls in a future article.why bother using any of those techniques :as the system scales up or single instance database will not be able to handle all of the request efficiently, which will cause reduce the response time , that will affect the user experience which in of itself will affect our sales and user count, you may think no problem let’s just get a stronger pc that can handle those request , but as we keep scaling up the hardware will be costly and hard to find (you’ll reach a point when you have the most powerful disk out there to run you db on ),for that reason we use this strategies to keep or response time and overall experience smooth no matter how much our app scales. Final ThoughtsNow that you have a fundamental understanding of replication and sharding, you can explore their trade-offs and edge cases in greater detail. Each technique has its own benefits and challenges, and the best approach depends on your system’s requirements.Stay tuned for more in-depth articles on advanced replication strategies, handling conflicts in distributed databases, and optimizing sharding techniques!

item.title: NestJS message bus vs @nestjs/microservices for handling RabbitMQ messages
item.author: Sebastian Iwanczyszyn
item.content:encoded: Building scalable applications requires efficient inter-service communication. NestJS provides multiple approaches for handling messages. In this article, we will compare these two methods and explore their strengths and limitations when working with RabbitMQ.What is NestJS Microservices with RMQ Transport Layer?NestJS provides built-in support for microservices architecture using the package. This allows applications to communicate asynchronously through various transport layers, including RabbitMQ (RMQ). This approach is particularly useful when starting an application as a worker service, without an HTTP server, focusing solely on processing messages from a queue.Example: Setting Up a Microservice with RabbitMQimport { NestFactory } from '@nestjs/core';import { MicroserviceOptions, Transport } from '@nestjs/microservices';import { AppModule } from './app.module';async function bootstrap() { const app = await NestFactory.createMicroservice<MicroserviceOptions>(AppModule, { transport: Transport.RMQ, options: { urls: ['amqp://localhost:5672'], queue: 'main_queue', durable: false, },}Handling Incoming Messagesimport { Controller } from '@nestjs/common';import { MessagePattern } from '@nestjs/microservices';export class OrdersService { @MessagePattern('order_created') handleOrderCreated(data: any) { console.log('Order received:', data); }This setup works well, but let’s challenge this approach. By default, the transport layer relies on AMQP default exchanges in RabbitMQ, which can be problematic in projects involving multiple programming languages that need to send messages over different exchanges.Sending a message over ClientProxy (Producer)import { Injectable } from '@nestjs/common';import { ClientProxy, ClientProxyFactory, Transport } from '@nestjs/microservices';export class MessagingService { private client: ClientProxy; this.client = ClientProxyFactory.create({ transport: Transport.RMQ, options: { urls: ['amqp://localhost:5672'], queue: 'my_app.event', durable: true, }, } async sendMessage(pattern: string, data: any) { return this.client.send(pattern, data); }This setup results in a strict message format requirement:{“pattern”:{“hello”:”hello!”},”data”:”message.pattern”}CQRS at the @nestjs/microservices Level?For applications leveraging CQRS, integrating with can be cumbersome. Messages from queues must be explicitly bound to commands or events and then routed to specific handlers. While NestJS provides tools to facilitate CQRS, ensuring a smooth and scalable implementation across multiple microservices is challenging. Every microservice would require a manual setup to handle message delegation effectively.Introducing @nestjstools/messagingTo overcome these challenges, offers a more flexible and scalable approach to messaging. This is a library designed for communication between microservices in a distributed system. The primary goal is to create faster, more maintainable microservices with better control over message routing.With just a simple configuration, we can define multiple message buses, queues, and exchanges:import { Module } from '@nestjs/common';import { MessagingRabbitmqExtensionModule } from '@nestjstools/messaging-rabbitmq-extension';import { AmqpChannelConfig, ExchangeType, InMemoryChannelConfig, MessagingModule } from '@nestjstools/messaging'; imports: [ MessagingRabbitmqExtensionModule, MessagingModule.forRoot({ { channels: ['memory-channel'], { channels: ['async-event'], { channels: ['async-command'], ], new InMemoryChannelConfig({ middlewares: [], avoidErrorsForNotExistedHandlers: true, }), name: 'async-command', connectionUri: 'amqp://guest:guest@localhost:5672/', exchangeName: 'my_app_command.exchange', bindingKeys: ['my_app_command.#'], exchangeType: ExchangeType.TOPIC, avoidErrorsForNotExistedHandlers: false, enableConsumer: true, new AmqpChannelConfig({ connectionUri: 'amqp://guest:guest@localhost:5672/', exchangeName: 'my_app_event.exchange', bindingKeys: ['my_app_event.#'], exchangeType: ExchangeType.TOPIC, queue: 'my_app.event', avoidErrorsForNotExistedHandlers: true, autoCreate: true, }), debug: true, ], providers: [],export class MessagingRmqModule {}Dispatching message via defined CommandBus@Controller()export class AppController { @MessageBus('command.bus') private commandBus: IMessageBus, createUserAsync(): string { this.commandBus.dispatch(new RoutingMessage(new CreateUser('John FROM Rabbit'), 'my_app_command.create_user')); }}Message Handler & Injected defined EventBusimport { CreateUser } from '../create-user';import { IMessageBus, IMessageHandler, MessageBus, MessageHandler, RoutingMessage } from '@nestjstools/messaging';import { UserCreated } from '../../event/user-created';@MessageHandler('my_app_command.create_user')export class CreateUserHandler implements IMessageHandler<CreateUser>{ @MessageBus('event.bus') private readonly eventBus: IMessageBus, } handle(message: CreateUser): Promise<object | void> { // TODO Logic there console.log(`Creating user... ${message.name}`); this.eventBus.dispatch(new RoutingMessage(new UserCreated(message.name), 'my_app_event.user_created')) return Promise.resolve(); }Why Use @nestjstools/messaging?✅ Multiple queue bindings at once: Manage various queues efficiently without redundancy.✅ : Configure your exchanges to meet your application’s specific needs, allowing for greater flexibility beyond the default AMQP exchanges.✅ Define binding keys dynamically: Use migrations or manual settings for flexibility in binding keys.✅ Enable/disable consumers per channel: Control consumer activation based on the application’s architecture. You can enable them on or separate Additionally, allows you to write custom and . This means you can customize the message structure or processing logic to fit your application’s specific requirements, enhancing the flexibility and maintainability of your microservices.Avoid Specific Message Formats to Handle MessagesOne major drawback of using @nestjs/microservices with RabbitMQ is that it enforces a specific message format. Every message must include a pattern and data field, complicating interoperability with other languages like Java or .NET. For example, a Java service would have to manually structure every message to match NestJS’s expectations. This lack of consistency across different services can lead to increased complexity and maintenance overhead.With , we can avoid this limitation and send messages more cleanly without being bound to a rigid format.Define Channels Based on What You NeedThe channel is your transport layer. One bus can send multiple messages to different services simultaneously. This flexibility allows for more efficient and scalable event-driven architectures.The Concept of Message BusThe concept of a is not something new! The library draws inspiration from several established frameworks, including:NServiceBus from .NET, known for its robust messaging capabilities.Symfony Messenger from PHP, which provides a versatile message bus for handling asynchronous messages.Axon Framework from Java, primarily focused on event sourcing but still incorporated the concept of a message bus for command and event handling.These influences have shaped the design of , making it a powerful tool for modern microservices architecture.When considering message handling in NestJS applications, both and have their unique advantages. While is a powerful tool for building microservices, it is built at a lower level and typically requires additional wrapping or configuration to function effectively as a comprehensive messaging solution. On the other hand, provides greater flexibility, scalability, and ease of integration, especially in complex systems requiring various message formats and routing strategies. Ultimately, the choice depends on your application’s specific needs and architecture.Thank you for taking the time to read this article! I hope the information provided has helped you in choosing the right library to meet your needs. If you have any further questions or need assistance, feel free to reach out. Happy coding, and best of luck with your projects!#Rabbitmq #NestJS #MessageBus #ServiceBus #DistributedSystems #microservices #NestJS-microservices

item.title: Exchange Types in RabbitMQ with Scala
item.author: Asela Maduwantha
item.content:encoded: RabbitMQ is a powerful open-source message broker that enables communication between distributed applications. One of its key features is the , which routes messages to queues based on different rules. In this blog, we’ll explore the four main exchange types in RabbitMQ with Scala examples.An is a routing mechanism in RabbitMQ that determines how messages are delivered to queues. When a producer sends a message, it first reaches an exchange, which then forwards it to one or more queues based on the exchange type and routing rules.2. Exchange Types in RabbitMQRabbitMQ supports four main types of exchanges:Let’s explore each with Scala examples.A routes messages to queues based on an exact match.import com.rabbitmq.client.{ConnectionFactory, Channel}object DirectExchangeProducer { def main(args: Array[String]): Unit = { val factory = new ConnectionFactory() factory.setHost("localhost") val connection = factory.newConnection() val channel: Channel = connection.createChannel() val exchangeName = "direct_logs" channel.exchangeDeclare(exchangeName, "direct") val message = "Error log message" channel.basicPublish(exchangeName, severity, null, message.getBytes("UTF-8")) println(s"[x] Sent '$severity':'$message'") connection.close()}object DirectExchangeConsumer { def main(args: Array[String]): Unit = { val factory = new ConnectionFactory() factory.setHost("localhost") val connection = factory.newConnection() val channel = connection.createChannel() val exchangeName = "direct_logs" val queueName = channel.queueDeclare().getQueue channel.queueBind(queueName, exchangeName, "error") println(s"[*] Waiting for messages in $queueName") val deliverCallback = (consumerTag: String, delivery: Delivery) => { val message = new String(delivery.getBody, "UTF-8") println(s"[x] Received '$message'") } channel.basicConsume(queueName, true, deliverCallback, _ => {}) }A broadcasts messages to all bound queues, regardless of the routing key.object FanoutExchangeProducer { def main(args: Array[String]): Unit = { val factory = new ConnectionFactory() factory.setHost("localhost") val connection = factory.newConnection() val channel = connection.createChannel() val exchangeName = "logs" channel.exchangeDeclare(exchangeName, "fanout") val message = "Broadcast message" channel.basicPublish(exchangeName, "", null, message.getBytes("UTF-8")) println(s"[x] Sent '$message'") connection.close()}object FanoutExchangeConsumer { def main(args: Array[String]): Unit = { val factory = new ConnectionFactory() factory.setHost("localhost") val connection = factory.newConnection() val channel = connection.createChannel() val exchangeName = "logs" val queueName = channel.queueDeclare().getQueue channel.queueBind(queueName, exchangeName, "") println(s"[*] Waiting for messages in $queueName") val deliverCallback = (consumerTag: String, delivery: Delivery) => { val message = new String(delivery.getBody, "UTF-8") println(s"[x] Received '$message'") } channel.basicConsume(queueName, true, deliverCallback, _ => {}) }A routes messages based on wildcard routing keys(wild card pattern matching).object TopicExchangeProducer { def main(args: Array[String]): Unit = { val factory = new ConnectionFactory() factory.setHost("localhost") val connection = factory.newConnection() val channel = connection.createChannel() val exchangeName = "topic_logs" channel.exchangeDeclare(exchangeName, "topic") val routingKey = "error.critical" val message = "Critical error log" channel.basicPublish(exchangeName, routingKey, null, message.getBytes("UTF-8")) println(s"[x] Sent '$routingKey':'$message'") connection.close()}object TopicExchangeConsumer { def main(args: Array[String]): Unit = { val factory = new ConnectionFactory() factory.setHost("localhost") val connection = factory.newConnection() val channel = connection.createChannel() val exchangeName = "topic_logs" val queueName = channel.queueDeclare().getQueue channel.queueBind(queueName, exchangeName, "error.*") println(s"[*] Waiting for messages in $queueName") val deliverCallback = (consumerTag: String, delivery: Delivery) => { val message = new String(delivery.getBody, "UTF-8") println(s"[x] Received '$message'") } channel.basicConsume(queueName, true, deliverCallback, _ => {}) }A routes messages based on custom header attributes instead of routing keys.object HeadersExchangeProducer { def main(args: Array[String]): Unit = { val factory = new ConnectionFactory() factory.setHost("localhost") val connection = factory.newConnection() val channel = connection.createChannel() val exchangeName = "headers_logs" channel.exchangeDeclare(exchangeName, "headers") val headers = Map("format" -> "pdf", "type" -> "report").asJava val props = new AMQP.BasicProperties.Builder().headers(headers).build() val message = "PDF Report Data" channel.basicPublish(exchangeName, "", props, message.getBytes("UTF-8")) println(s"[x] Sent message with headers: $headers") connection.close()}object HeadersExchangeConsumer { def main(args: Array[String]): Unit = { val factory = new ConnectionFactory() factory.setHost("localhost") val connection = factory.newConnection() val channel = connection.createChannel() val exchangeName = "headers_logs" val queueName = channel.queueDeclare().getQueue val headers = Map("format" -> "pdf").asJava channel.queueBind(queueName, exchangeName, "", headers) println(s"[*] Waiting for messages in $queueName") channel.basicConsume(queueName, true, deliverCallback, _ => {})}RabbitMQ exchanges provide flexible message routing mechanisms that help in building scalable and decoupled applications. Understanding , , , and exchanges allows developers to choose the best pattern based on their messaging needs. By using Scala with RabbitMQ, you can efficiently implement these messaging patterns in your applications, ensuring reliable communication between distributed services.

item.title: Deploying Elasticsearch, Fluentbit, and Kibana (EFK) on Kubernetes
item.author: Muhammad Abdullah
item.content:encoded: Efficient log management is crucial for monitoring and troubleshooting applications running on Kubernetes. The EFK stack — Elasticsearch, Fluentbit, and Kibana — offers a powerful solution for log aggregation, processing, and visualization. In this article, we’ll walk through the deployment of the EFK stack on a Kubernetes cluster.Before we begin, ensure you have the following:A running Kubernetes clusterkubectl configured to manage your clusterStep 1: Deploy ElasticsearchElasticsearch is the storage and search engine for logs.Deploy Elasticsearch using a StatefulSetapiVersion: apps/v1kind: StatefulSet name: elasticsearchspec: serviceName: "elasticsearch" replicas: 3 matchLabels: template: labels: spec: nodeAffinity: requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution: nodeSelectorTerms: - key: kubernetes.io/hostname values: - node01 containers: image: docker.elastic.co/elasticsearch/elasticsearch:8.13.0 - containerPort: 9200 - containerPort: 9300 env: value: single-node - name: xpack.security.enabled value: "false" - name: es-data mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/data initContainers: - name: create-directories image: busybox command: ["sh", "-c", "mkdir -p /usr/share/elasticsearch/data && chown -R 1000:1000 /usr/share/elasticsearch/data"] securityContext: volumeMounts: mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/data - metadata: spec: accessModes: [ "ReadWriteOnce" ] resources: storage: 5GiDeploy PersistentVolumes for ElasticsearchapiVersion: v1kind: PersistentVolume name: pv-elasticsearch-controlplane capacity: accessModes: hostPath: path: /data/elasticsearch-controlplane storageClassName: manualapiVersion: v1metadata: name: pv-elasticsearch-node01spec: storage: 5Gi - ReadWriteOnce path: /data/elasticsearch-node01---kind: PersistentVolume name: pv-elasticsearch-node02 capacity: accessModes: hostPath: path: /data/elasticsearch-node02 storageClassName: manualFluentbit is a lightweight log processor and forwarder, sending logs to Elasticsearch.Deploy Fluentbit Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1kind: ClusterRole name: fluent-bit app: fluent-bit- apiGroups: resources: verbs: ["*"]apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1kind: ClusterRoleBinding name: fluent-bit kind: ClusterRole apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io- kind: ServiceAccount namespace: efkKibana provides a web interface for visualizing logs stored in Elasticsearch.apiVersion: apps/v1kind: Deployment name: kibanaspec: selector: app: kibana metadata: app: kibana containers: image: docker.elastic.co/kibana/kibana:8.13.0 - containerPort: 5601 - name: ELASTICSEARCH_HOSTS value: "http://elasticsearch:9200" volumeMounts: mountPath: /usr/share/kibana/config - name: kibana-config name: kibana-configStep 4: Scaling the EFK StackTo scale Elasticsearch, increase the number of replicas in the StatefulSet:For Fluentbit, ensure it runs as a DaemonSet so that it scales automatically with new nodes.For Kibana, increase the number of replicas in the Deployment:By deploying and scaling the EFK stack on Kubernetes, you gain powerful log aggregation and visualization capabilities, making troubleshooting easier. This setup can be further customized with security policies and additional filters based on your logging needs.

item.title: Kubernetes 101: A Beginner’s Guide to Container Orchestration
item.author: Okan Yenigün
item.content:encoded: Getting Started with Kubernetes: Core Concepts ExplainedKubernetes, often abbreviated as , is an open-source system designed to automate the deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications. Google originally developed it and now it is widely used across various industries.Kubernetes manages the scheduling and operation of containers across a cluster of machines, ensuring optimal resource utilization and high availability.So, what is a container? You can check out my Docker post:Containers are lightweight, standalone, and executable packages that include everything needed to run a piece of software, including the code, runtime, libraries, and dependencies. Docker is the most popular container runtime, but Kubernetes also supports other runtimes.When you have a few containers, managing them manually is manageable. But when you have dozens, hundreds, or even thousands, it becomes a nightmare. Kubernetes automates the deployment, scaling, and management of these containers.Kubernetes offers a rich set of features:It manages the deployment and operation of containers across a cluster of nodes. It ensures containers are running where and when they’re supposed to, handling the complexity of distributing them efficiently.It supports both horizontal and vertical scaling.Horizontal Pod Autoscaling (HPA) adjusts the number of pods based on metrics like CPU or memory usage, while Vertical Pod Autoscaling (VPA) tweaks resource limits for individual pods. There’s also Cluster Autoscaling, which adjusts the number of nodes based on workload demands.Kubernetes keeps applications running smoothly by automatically restarting containers that fail, rescheduling them if nodes go down, and replacing or killing containers that don’t response to health checks. This ensures high availability.It supports zero-downtime deployments by gradually updating pods with new versions and rolling back to a previous state if something goes wrong, all without interrupting service.Kubernetes cluster is a collection of machines (nodes) that work together to run containerized applications.The Control Plane (left side), also named the Master Node, is responsible for managing the entire Kubernetes cluster.We don’t run our applications on the master node; it functions more like the brain of Kubernetes. is the front door to Kubernetes. It’s the entry point where all requests come in, get processed, and are directed to the right place. It enforces authentication, authorization, and admission control.The assigns pods to nodes based on resource availability and constraints.The runs various controller processes that regulate the state of the cluster. Each controller monitors the state of different components and works to align the current state with the desired state in the cluster’s configuration.The is a distributed key-value store used as Kubernetes’ primary database. It stores all cluster data, including configuration, state, and metadata. It acts as the source of truth for the cluster’s state. If the is lost or corrupted, the cluster cannot function.We deploy our application to nodes. Each worker node runs application workloads and communicates with the control plane.Each worker node has a , the agent running on each node, responsible for ensuring containers are running as expected and communicating with the control plane.Additionally, each node has a .: Maintains network rules on the node, allowing network communication to podsA is the smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes. It is an abstraction that encapsulates one or more containers, storage, network configuration, and metadata.Think of a pod as a wrapper around containers that provide:A shared network namespace (all containers inside a pod share the same IP and port space).A unit for scheduling and resource allocation.Each worker Node in Kubernetes can run multiple Pods.A Pod can have one or more containers that share resources like storage and networking, usually one application per pod is preferred.Kubernetes decides which Node a Pod will run on based on resource availability. is a Kubernetes API object that manages external access to services, typically via HTTP/HTTPS routers. For example;The request reaches the Ingress controller. The controller checks the Ingress rules to determine which service should handle the request.The expose the applications to allow internal and external communication.The request is routed to the some-app service, which then directs it to the appropriate pod running the application.If the application needs to fetch data, it communicates with the DB service internally for instance.A is used to store non-sensitive configuration data separately from the application code. It allows you to decouple environment-specific configurations (such as database connection strings, API keys, or feature flags) from containerized applications.Ingress exposes the application Hollow App Pod within the cluster, making it accessible to users.ConfigMap stores the database connection string that the application requires to communicate with the database. The Hollow App Container reads this connection string, and then applies it to establish communication with the database.On the contrary, a in Kubernetes is designed to store sensitive information such as passwords, API keys, tokens, and certificates. Unlike ConfigMaps, Secrets are stored in an encoded format and are not exposed in plain text. are used to provide persistent storage to containers within a Pod. Unlike container-local storage, which is ephemeral and lost when a container restarts, volumes persist data throughout the Pod’s lifecycle.We have different workload controllers: Deployment, StatefulSet, and DaemonSet. is used for stateless applications where Pods are interchangeable and do not require stable network identities or persistent storage. Pods are created with random names. is used for stateful applications, ensuring Pods maintain a stable identity and persistent storage. Pods are created in a strict order. ensures that one Pod (a copy) runs on every (or specific) Nodes in the cluster.Minikube is an open-source tool that enables you to run a local Kubernetes cluster on your personal computer.It is lightweight and allows us yo run a single-node Kubernetes cluster. It is mainly used for learning, development and testing Kubernetes applications without needing a full production cluster.You can download it from:After downloading, let’s start it.This will start a cluster on my machine.okan@yeniguno:~$ minikube start😄 minikube v1.35.0 on Ubuntu 24.04✨ Using the docker driver based on existing profile💨 For improved Docker performance, enable the overlay Linux kernel module using 'modprobe overlay'⛔ Requested memory allocation (1833MB) is less than the recommended minimum 1900MB. Deployments may fail.👍 Starting "minikube" primary control-plane node in "minikube" cluster🚜 Pulling base image v0.0.46 ...🏃 Updating the running docker "minikube" container ...🐳 Preparing Kubernetes v1.32.0 on Docker 27.4.1 ...🔎 Verifying Kubernetes components... ▪ Using image gcr.io/k8s-minikube/storage-provisioner:v5🌟 Enabled addons: storage-provisioner, default-storageclass🏄 Done! kubectl is now configured to use "minikube" cluster and "default" namespace by defaultDownload Docker Desktop if you don’t have it already:Next, we need to download .Kubernetes Control (kubectl) is the command-line interface for managing Kubernetes clusters.Client Version: v1.32.2Kustomize Version: v5.5.0List all Pods in cluster:NAME STATUS ROLES AGE VERSIONminikube Ready control-plane 3m59s v1.32.0We have a single node which is the control-plane node.List all Pods across all namespaces:NAMESPACE NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGEkube-system coredns-668d6bf9bc-fqvbt 1/1 Running 1 (117s ago) 5m4skube-system etcd-minikube 1/1 Running 1 (2m2s ago) 5m9skube-system kube-apiserver-minikube 1/1 Running 1 (112s ago) 5m9skube-system kube-controller-manager-minikube 1/1 Running 1 (2m2s ago) 5m9skube-system kube-proxy-wj4tm 1/1 Running 1 (2m2s ago) 5m5skube-system kube-scheduler-minikube 1/1 Running 1 (2m2s ago) 5m9skube-system storage-provisioner 1/1 Running 3 (108s ago) 5m8sWe see the system pods running on the control plane.Let’s create a Deployment: nginx-deployment.yamlapiVersion: apps/v1kind: Deployment name: nginx-deployment app: nginx replicas: 3 matchLabels: template: labels: spec: - name: nginx ports:This YAML file defines a Kubernetes Deployment that manages an Nginx web server. It ensures that 3 replicas of the Nginx Pod are running at all times.apiVersion: apps/v1kind: DeploymentapiVersion: apps/v1 → Specifies the Kubernetes API version used.kind: Deployment → Defines this resource as a Deployment, which manages Pods and ensures high availability.metadata: name: nginx-deployment app: nginxname: nginx-deployment → The name of the Deployment.labels: app: nginx → Labels help in identifying and selecting resources (useful for selectors).3. Specification of the Deployment (spec)replicas: 3 → Kubernetes will run 3 replicas (instances) of the Nginx Pod to ensure availability and load balancing.4. Selector (Matching Pods)selector: matchLabels:matchLabels: app: nginx → This Deployment will manage Pods that have the label app: nginx.Ensures only Pods with this label are controlled by this Deployment.template: metadata: app: nginxDefines the Pod specification (what each Pod should look like).The label app: nginx ensures Pods are correctly identified and managed.spec: containers: image: nginx:1.14.2 - containerPort: 80Defines the container inside each Pod.name: nginx → The container's name.image: nginx:1.14.2 → Pulls the Nginx 1.14.2 image from Docker Hub.ports: containerPort: 80 → The container will listen on port 80 (default HTTP port).Creates a Deployment named nginx-deployment.Ensures 3 Nginx Pods are always running.Labels Pods with app: nginx so they can be managed and selected.Runs Nginx 1.14.2 in a container inside each Pod.Exposes port 80 inside each container for HTTP traffic.We apply the development:kubectl apply -f nginx-deployment.yamldeployment.apps/nginx-deployment createdWe can check running pods:NAMESPACE NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGEdefault nginx-deployment-647677fc66-g6kh8 1/1 Running 0 51sdefault nginx-deployment-647677fc66-mvftc 1/1 Running 0 51sdefault nginx-deployment-647677fc66-zpsfr 1/1 Running 0 51skube-system coredns-668d6bf9bc-fqvbt 1/1 Running 1 (7m27s ago) 10mkube-system etcd-minikube 1/1 Running 1 (7m32s ago) 10mkube-system kube-apiserver-minikube 1/1 Running 1 (7m22s ago) 10mkube-system kube-controller-manager-minikube 1/1 Running 1 (7m32s ago) 10mkube-system kube-proxy-wj4tm 1/1 Running 1 (7m32s ago) 10mkube-system kube-scheduler-minikube 1/1 Running 1 (7m32s ago) 10mkube-system storage-provisioner 1/1 Running 3 (7m18s ago) 10mWe see three new pods, nginx-deployment-…, in the ContainerCreating status.kubectl get deployments --all-namespacesNAMESPACE NAME READY UP-TO-DATE AVAILABLE AGEdefault nginx-deployment 3/3 3 3 2m6skube-system coredns 1/1 1 1 11mWe created a deployment that contains three pods.Let’s change something in the Deployment configuration, for example the replicas: kubectl apply -f nginx-deployment.yamlNAMESPACE NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGEdefault nginx-deployment-647677fc66-97n5x 1/1 Running 0 2sdefault nginx-deployment-647677fc66-g6kh8 1/1 Running 0 4m9sdefault nginx-deployment-647677fc66-mvftc 1/1 Running 0 4m9sdefault nginx-deployment-647677fc66-pw2zs 1/1 Running 0 2sdefault nginx-deployment-647677fc66-tt7zm 1/1 Running 0 2sdefault nginx-deployment-647677fc66-zpsfr 1/1 Running 0 4m9skube-system coredns-668d6bf9bc-fqvbt 1/1 Running 1 (10m ago) 13mkube-system etcd-minikube 1/1 Running 1 (10m ago) 13mkube-system kube-apiserver-minikube 1/1 Running 1 (10m ago) 13mkube-system kube-controller-manager-minikube 1/1 Running 1 (10m ago) 13mkube-system kube-proxy-wj4tm 1/1 Running 1 (10m ago) 13mkube-system kube-scheduler-minikube 1/1 Running 1 (10m ago) 13mkube-system storage-provisioner 1/1 Running 3 (10m ago) 13mLens is a popular open-source IDE specifically designed for managing and troubleshooting Kubernetes clusters. It provides developers and DevOps engineers with a comprehensive and intuitive interface to visualize and control all aspects of their Kubernetes environments.This is a simple app that uses an image from Docker Hub:It uses MongoDB and a simple web interface.First, we create the mongo-config.yaml file. This file defines a , which stores non-sensitive configuration data for the MongoDB service.apiVersion: v1kind: ConfigMap name: mongo-config mongo-url: mongo-serviceCreates a ConfigMap named mongo-config.Stores the MongoDB service URL as mongo-url: mongo-service.Used by the web application to connect to the MongoDB service.Second, we create mongo-secret.yaml file. This file defines a , which stores sensitive credentials for the MongoDB database.apiVersion: v1kind: Secret name: mongo-secretdata: mongo-password: YWRtaW4xMjM=Creates a Kubernetes Secret named mongo-secret.Stores the MongoDB username and password in an encoded format.Provides authentication credentials for the MongoDB container and the web application.The user and the password are Base64-encoded values:echo -n admin123 | base64Next, we create mongo.yaml file. This file defines both a (for running a MongoDB database) and a (for exposing MongoDB within the Kubernetes cluster).apiVersion: apps/v1kind: Deployment name: mongo-deployment app: mongo replicas: 1 matchLabels: template: labels: spec: - name: mongodb ports: env: - name: MONGO_INITDB_ROOT_USERNAME valueFrom: name: mongo-secret - name: MONGO_INITDB_ROOT_PASSWORD secretKeyRef: key: mongo-passwordapiVersion: v1metadata:spec: app: mongo - protocol: TCP targetPort: 27017apiVersion: apps/v1kind: Deployment name: mongo-deployment app: mongoDefines a Deployment named mongo-deployment.Uses the label app: mongo, which helps identify this resource.Pod Replicas & Matching Selectorspec: replicas: 1 matchLabels:replicas: 1 → Runs one MongoDB instance.Selector (matchLabels) ensures that only Pods with app: mongo are managed by this Deployment.template: metadata: app: mongoThe Pod will have the label app: mongo, allowing it to be selected by the Service.MongoDB Container Configurationcontainers:- name: mongodb ports:Runs the MongoDB 5.0 Docker image.Exposes port 27017, which is MongoDB’s default port.Environment Variables for Credentialsenv:- name: MONGO_INITDB_ROOT_USERNAME secretKeyRef: key: mongo-user- name: MONGO_INITDB_ROOT_PASSWORD valueFrom: name: mongo-secretMongoDB requires a username and password for authentication.It fetches credentials securely from the Kubernetes Secret (mongo-secret).---apiVersion: v1metadata:spec: app: mongo - protocol: TCP targetPort: 27017apiVersion: v1kind: Service name: mongo-serviceDefines a named mongo-service, which other apps can use to access MongoDB.Selector for MongoDB PodsSelects Pods labeled app: mongo, ensuring it routes traffic to the MongoDB Pod.ports: - protocol: TCP targetPort: 27017port: 27017 → The Service listens on port 27017.targetPort: 27017 → Routes traffic to the MongoDB container on port 27017.Finally, we create webapp.yaml file. This file defines both a (for running a web application) and a (for exposing the web app to users).apiVersion: apps/v1kind: Deployment name: webapp-deployment app: webapp replicas: 1 matchLabels: template: labels: spec: - name: webapp image: nanajanashia/k8s-demo-app:v1.0 ports: env: valueFrom: name: mongo-secret - name: USER_PWD secretKeyRef: key: mongo-password valueFrom: name: mongo-config---kind: Service name: webapp-service type: NodePort app: webapp - protocol: TCP targetPort: 3000Creates a Deployment (webapp-deployment) that runs a web application inside a Pod.Uses MongoDB credentials from the Secret (mongo-secret) to connect securely.Fetches the MongoDB service URL from the ConfigMap (mongo-config).Creates a Service (webapp-service) that exposes the web app via NodePort, making it accessible externally.Before starting, let’s delete the pods created earlier in the nginx-deployment.kubectl delete deployment nginx-deploymentdeployment.apps "nginx-deployment" deletedNo resources found in default namespace.Now we deploy this app using apply command.kubectl apply -f mongo-config.yamlconfigmap/mongo-config createdkubectl apply -f mongo-secret.yamlkubectl apply -f mongo.yamldeployment.apps/mongo-deployment createdservice/mongo-service createdkubectl apply -f webapp.yaml deployment.apps/webapp-deployment createdservice/webapp-service createdLet’s retrieve all Kubernetes resources in the current namespace.NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGEpod/mongo-deployment-7cccf8b6d8-rn2px 1/1 Running 0 66spod/webapp-deployment-6bb4795f54-vrt54 0/1 ContainerCreating 0 39sNAME TYPE CLUSTER-IP EXTERNAL-IP PORT(S) AGEservice/kubernetes ClusterIP 10.96.0.1 <none> 443/TCP 4h41mservice/mongo-service ClusterIP 10.96.7.226 <none> 27017/TCP 66sservice/webapp-service NodePort 10.103.158.97 <none> 3000:30100/TCP 39sNAME READY UP-TO-DATE AVAILABLE AGEdeployment.apps/mongo-deployment 1/1 1 1 66sdeployment.apps/webapp-deployment 0/1 1 0 39sNAME DESIRED CURRENT READY AGEreplicaset.apps/mongo-deployment-7cccf8b6d8 1 1 1 66sreplicaset.apps/webapp-deployment-6bb4795f54 1 1 0 39sWe can get ConfigMaps and Secrets as well.NAME DATA AGEkube-root-ca.crt 1 4h41mmongo-config 1 2m44sNAME TYPE DATA AGEmongo-secret Opaque 2 2m40sNAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGEmongo-deployment-7cccf8b6d8-rn2px 1/1 Running 0 2m39swebapp-deployment-6bb4795f54-vrt54 1/1 Running 0 2m12sWe can get the details of a service using describe command.kubectl describe service webapp-serviceName: webapp-serviceNamespace: defaultLabels: <none>Annotations: <none>Selector: app=webappType: NodePortIP Family Policy: SingleStackIP Families: IPv4IP: 10.103.158.97IPs: 10.103.158.97Port: <unset> 3000/TCPTargetPort: 3000/TCPNodePort: <unset> 30100/TCPEndpoints: 10.244.0.37:3000Session Affinity: NoneExternal Traffic Policy: ClusterInternal Traffic Policy: ClusterEvents: <none>kubectl logs webapp-deployment-6bb4795f54-vrt54app listening on port 3000!NAME TYPE CLUSTER-IP EXTERNAL-IP PORT(S) AGEkubernetes ClusterIP 10.96.0.1 <none> 443/TCP 4h45mmongo-service ClusterIP 10.96.7.226 <none> 27017/TCP 5m28swebapp-service NodePort 10.103.158.97 <none> 3000:30100/TCP 5m1sNAME STATUS ROLES AGE VERSION INTERNAL-IP EXTERNAL-IP OS-IMAGE KERNEL-VERSION CONTAINER-RUNTIMEminikube Ready control-plane 4h46m v1.32.0 192.168.49.2 <none> Ubuntu 22.04.5 LTS 6.12.5-linuxkit docker://27.4.1To run the application using minikube:minikube service webapp-service|-----------|----------------|-------------|---------------------------|| NAMESPACE | NAME | TARGET PORT | URL ||-----------|----------------|-------------|---------------------------|| default | webapp-service | 3000 | http://192.168.49.2:30100 ||-----------|----------------|-------------|---------------------------|🏃 Starting tunnel for service webapp-service.|-----------|----------------|-------------|------------------------|| NAMESPACE | NAME | TARGET PORT | URL ||-----------|----------------|-------------|------------------------|| default | webapp-service | | http://127.0.0.1:45947 ||-----------|----------------|-------------|------------------------|🎉 Opening service default/webapp-service in default browser...❗ Because you are using a Docker driver on linux, the terminal needs to be open to run it.Gtk-Message: 18:18:27.010: Not loading module "atk-bridge": The functionality is provided by GTK natively. Please try to not load it.[20414, Main Thread] WARNING: GTK+ module /snap/firefox/5134/gnome-platform/usr/lib/gtk-2.0/modules/libcanberra-gtk-module.so cannot be loaded.GTK+ 2.x symbols detected. Using GTK+ 2.x and GTK+ 3 in the same process is not supported.: 'glib warning', file /build/firefox/parts/firefox/build/toolkit/xre/nsSigHandlers.cpp:187(firefox:20414): Gtk-WARNING **: 18:18:27.107: GTK+ module /snap/firefox/5134/gnome-platform/usr/lib/gtk-2.0/modules/libcanberra-gtk-module.so cannot be loaded.GTK+ 2.x symbols detected. Using GTK+ 2.x and GTK+ 3 in the same process is not supported.Gtk-Message: 18:18:27.107: Failed to load module "canberra-gtk-module"[20414, Main Thread] WARNING: GTK+ module /snap/firefox/5134/gnome-platform/usr/lib/gtk-2.0/modules/libcanberra-gtk-module.so cannot be loaded.GTK+ 2.x symbols detected. Using GTK+ 2.x and GTK+ 3 in the same process is not supported.: 'glib warning', file /build/firefox/parts/firefox/build/toolkit/xre/nsSigHandlers.cpp:187(firefox:20414): Gtk-WARNING **: 18:18:27.109: GTK+ module /snap/firefox/5134/gnome-platform/usr/lib/gtk-2.0/modules/libcanberra-gtk-module.so cannot be loaded.GTK+ 2.x symbols detected. Using GTK+ 2.x and GTK+ 3 in the same process is not supported.Gtk-Message: 18:18:27.109: Failed to load module "canberra-gtk-module"In conclusion, Kubernetes is important because it automates the deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications, ensuring they run efficiently and reliably.At its core, Kubernetes operates on the principle of declarative configuration — you define the desired state of your system, and Kubernetes works to maintain that state.As a beginner, keep in mind that Kubernetes is powerful but complex; focus on understanding its core components like Pods, Nodes, and Controllers, and remember that it’s designed to handle scalability and resilience for modern applications.

item.title: Scaling Azure Event Hub Processing Without Skyrocketing Costs
item.author: Noel
item.content:encoded: Azure Event Hub is a high-throughput, event ingestion service built to handle millions of events per second. It is widely used in IoT telemetry, real-time analytics, distributed event-driven architectures, and microservices orchestration. However, while Event Hub can scale effortlessly, improper configuration can result in excessive storage and compute costs, particularly when dealing with high-frequency triggers, checkpointing overhead, and transactional storage operations.For organizations handling real-time event streaming at scale, optimizing Event Hub processing isn’t just a cost concern but also a performance and reliability challenge. Without the right configurations, enterprises may find themselves paying for unnecessary transactions, overloading storage accounts, and introducing latency into mission-critical event pipelines.In this article, we provide a technical deep dive into the best practices our Azure specialists adopt at Xinthe, for scaling Azure Event Hub processing while minimizing storage and compute costs.As you complete this article, you will hopefully have a clear, actionable strategy for scaling Event Hub processing efficiently, enabling your real-time analytics, IoT event pipelines, or microservices architecture to run with maximum cost-efficiency and performance.Understanding Azure Event Hub ArchitectureBefore optimizing Azure Event Hub for scalability and cost efficiency, it is critical to understand its core components and scaling mechanics. Event Hub operates as a distributed event streaming platform, designed to handle high-throughput workloads, but misconfigured scaling can lead to inefficiencies, high storage costs, and unnecessary compute expenses.Core Components Of Event HubAzure Event Hub is composed of four primary components -1. Producers (Event Publishers)Producers are applications, IoT devices, or microservices that send events into Event Hub. These can be -✔ IoT devices publishing telemetry data (temperature, humidity, GPS coordinates, etc.)✔ Application logs and metrics from distributed services✔ Clickstream data from web or mobile applications✔ Security event monitoring & real-time fraud detectionExample: Sending an Event to Event Hub Using Pythonfrom azure.eventhub import EventHubProducerClient, EventDataproducer = EventHubProducerClient.from_connection_string( "Endpoint=sb://myeventhubnamespace.servicebus.windows.net/", eventhub_name="my-event-hub") event_data_batch = producer.create_batch() event_data_batch.add(EventData("IoT device temperature: 72°F")) producer.send_batch(event_data_batch)Key Optimization Considerations For Producers before sending them to reduce the number of storage transactions.Use Event Hubs’ batch API to optimize network overhead and minimize costs.Manage event bursts efficiently to prevent throttling and unnecessary auto-scaling.Partitions are logical data streams inside Event Hub that allow for parallel processing of events.✔ Each partition acts as an independent log where events are stored in order.✔ Partitions enable horizontal scaling — each consumer only reads from assigned partitions.✔ Higher partition counts increase throughput but also add costs and complexity.Event Hub Partitioning ModelKey Optimization Considerations For PartitionsChoose an appropriate partition count based on ingestion rate.Avoid unnecessary partitioning — each partition has a cost in terms of scaling and consumer management. — imbalanced partitions cause bottlenecks.3. Consumers (Event Processors)Consumers are applications that read, process, and analyze events from Event Hub. Common consumers include -✔ — Serverless event-driven compute.✔ — Real-time data transformations.✔ Apache Spark On Azure Databricks — Distributed event processing.✔ — Event processing with .Reading Events from Event Hub Using Azure Functionimport azure.functions as funcdef main(event: func.EventHubEvent): message = event.get_body().decode('utf-8') print(f"Received event: {message}")Key Optimization Considerations For ConsumersOptimize batch size for efficient processing (discussed in later sections).Use multiple consumer groups for independent processing pipelines.Checkpoint less frequently to reduce Azure Storage transaction costs.4. Throughput Units (TUs) — The Capacity-Based Scaling ModelThroughput Units (TUs) define the ingestion and processing capacity of an Event Hub. Each TU provides -✔ 1 MB/sec ingress or 1,000 events/sec (whichever is higher).✔ 2 MB/sec egress across all consumers.✔ Up to 84 GB of event retention over a rolling 7-day window.Key Cost Considerations For Throughput UnitsTUs are billed per hour, regardless of usage — over-provisioning wastes resources.Using auto-inflate TU scaling prevents unnecessary costs.For unpredictable workloads, consider Event Hubs Premium or Dedicated tiers.How Scaling Works In Event HubScaling in Azure Event Hub must be optimized at both the producer and consumer levels to ensure cost-efficient, high-performance streaming.1. Scaling At The Producer Level — Managing Event Bursts EfficientlyMany workloads (especially IoT data ingestion) generate event bursts — sudden spikes in the number of messages sent. Without proper handling, this leads to overloaded TUs and unnecessary autoscaling costs.✔ Batch Events to Reduce Storage TransactionsInstead of sending individual events, batch multiple events together to minimize the number of transactions.Example: Sending Batched Events Efficientlybatch = producer.create_batch()for _ in range(100): batch.add(EventData("Temperature reading"))producer.send_batch(batch)✔ Leverage Event Hubs Auto-InflateEnabling auto-inflate allows Event Hub to dynamically scale TUs up and down based on demand.az eventhubs namespace update --name mynamespace --resource-group mygroup --enable-auto-inflate --maximum-throughput-units 20✔ Partition Load Balancing For Large Event StreamsAssign partitions dynamically to avoid hot spots.Use Partition Key routing to ensure even event distribution.2. Scaling At The Consumer Level — Distributing Processing Across PartitionsOnce events are ingested into Event Hub, they need to be efficiently processed to avoid latency and excessive storage transactions.✔ Optimizing Consumer Checkpointing FrequencyCheckpointing every event incurs massive Azure Storage costs. Instead -Checkpoint only after processing batches (e.g., every 500 events).Use in-memory state tracking before persisting to storage.✔ Using Multiple Consumer Groups For Parallel ProcessingConsumer groups allow multiple services to read the same data stream independently.
