Assigning Consumer Groups in Azure CLIaz eventhubs eventhub consumer-group create --name analyticsGroup --eventhub-name myeventhub --namespace-name mynamespace --resource-group mygroup✔ Scaling Event Processing With Apache Spark & DatabricksFor high-throughput, distributed event processing, Apache Spark on Azure Databricks is ideal.Example: Processing Event Hub Data in Azure Databricksfrom pyspark.sql import SparkSessionspark = SparkSession.builder.appName("EventHubProcessing").getOrCreate() .readStream \ .option("eventhubs.connectionString", "<CONNECTION_STRING>") \df.writeStream.format("console").start()Understanding Azure Event Hub’s architecture and scaling mechanics is the first step in optimizing performance and cost efficiency.Choosing The Right Event Hub Tier For Cost OptimizationSelecting the right Azure Event Hub pricing tier is essential for balancing performance and cost efficiency. Event Hub offers three tiers — Basic, Standard, and Dedicated — each suited for different workload types. Choosing the wrong tier can result in unnecessary spending or insufficient throughput, leading to bottlenecks.In this section, we break down the capabilities, limitations, and cost trade-offs of each tier to help you make an informed decision.Basic Vs. Standard Vs. Dedicated TiersAzure Event Hub pricing is tiered, with increasing capabilities and costs. Below is a breakdown of which tier best suits different workloads.1️⃣ Basic Tier — Ideal For Lightweight Logging & Development✔ Best for non-critical, low-throughput scenarios.✔ Limited to 1 consumer group per event hub.✔ No Kafka protocol support.✔ No auto-inflate scaling — must manually provision TUs.Development & Testing: Proof-of-concept applications.Lightweight Application Logging: Simple telemetry pipelines.Low-Traffic IoT Data Streams: Devices sending sparse data.Cheapest option but lacks scalability.Limited consumer groups restrict multi-application processing.No support for larger event streams or high-throughput workloads.💡 Use Basic only for development, lightweight monitoring, or small-scale event streaming.2️⃣ Standard Tier — Best For Production Workloads✔ Supports multiple consumer groups — ideal for analytics & multi-tenant event processing.✔ Supports Kafka-compatible event ingestion.✔ Auto-inflate throughput scaling for demand spikes.✔ Up to 20 throughput units (TUs) (can handle millions of events per second).✔ Supports event archiving with Azure Blob Storage.Use Cases For Standard TierReal-Time Analytics Pipelines (Azure Stream Analytics, Databricks).High-Volume IoT Event Processing (Millions of devices).Multi-Consumer Processing Pipelines (Different applications reading the same stream).TUs billed per hour — over-provisioning can lead to waste.Auto-inflate prevents under-provisioning but requires monitoring.Checkpointing frequency impacts storage transaction costs.💡 For most production workloads, the Standard Tier is the best balance between scalability, flexibility, and cost.3️⃣ Dedicated Tier — Enterprise-Grade Performance & Isolation✔ Provides isolated capacity — dedicated clusters with unlimited TUs.✔ Supports highly regulated industries needing guaranteed performance.✔ Fixed pricing model — no TU billing fluctuations.✔ Ideal for massive-scale workloads exceeding 20 million events per second.Use Cases For Dedicated TierMission-Critical Financial Services & Fraud Detection.Enterprise-Wide Telemetry & Observability Platforms.Large-Scale Video Streaming & CDN Event Processing.Fixed monthly pricing (~$4,000+ per Event Hub unit).Cheaper than scaling Standard Tier beyond 20+ TUs.Requires long-term commitment for cost efficiency.💡 Use Dedicated Tier only when sustained, enterprise-grade performance is required. Otherwise, scale Standard Tier strategically.Cost Trade-Offs — Standard Vs. Dedicated Pricing For Real-World Workloads1️⃣ Standard Tier Cost BreakdownEvent Hub Standard pricing is based on throughput units (TUs) and storage transactions.Example Cost For A Medium-Scale IoT Pipeline5 TUs (~500,000 events/sec) → $0.028 * 5 * 730 hours = $102/month.Checkpointing 100,000 times per hour (~3 million/day) → $27/month.10GB data egress per day → $24/month.Total Monthly Cost → ~$153/month.2️⃣ Dedicated Tier Cost BreakdownExample Cost For A Large-Scale Event Hub DeploymentHandling 2 million events/sec → Would require $1,000+/month).Fixed pricing makes Dedicated cheaper at higher scale (~$4,000/month).Reducing Costs By Efficiently Utilizing Throughput Units (TUs)Even within Standard Tier, optimizing TUs and storage transactions can significantly reduce costs.1️⃣ Use Auto-Inflate To Scale Dynamically💡 Instead of over-provisioning TUs, enable auto-inflate to scale dynamically.az eventhubs namespace update --name mynamespace --resource-group mygroup --enable-auto-inflate --maximum-throughput-units 20 Reduces unnecessary TUs during off-peak hours.2️⃣ Optimize Batch Size To Reduce Transaction Costs💡 Processing every event separately triggers excessive storage writes. Instead, batch events to reduce transaction count.Increasing Checkpoint Batch Sizeevent_processor_options = { "initial_offset": "earliest", "checkpoint_batch_size": 500 # Checkpoint every 500 messages instead of every event} Reduces checkpointing transactions by 99% in high-throughput workloads.3️⃣ Store Raw Events In Tiered Storage For Cost Savings💡 Instead of keeping all events in Event Hub for extended retention, archive them to Blob Storage or ADLS.az eventhubs eventhub update --name myeventhub --resource-group mygroup --enable-capture true --destination-name myblobstorage Azure Blob Storage is significantly cheaper than Event Hub retention for long-term storage.Final Thought: Picking the Right Event Hub Tier for Your WorkloadKey Takeaways For Cost Optimization✅ Use Standard Tier with auto-inflate unless you require dedicated resources.✅ Avoid checkpointing every event — batch and checkpoint efficiently.✅ Archive raw events to cheaper storage (Blob, ADLS) instead of keeping them in Event Hub.✅ Monitor TU utilization to prevent over-provisioning.Partitioning Strategies For Efficient ScalingPartitioning is one of the most critical aspects of scaling Azure Event Hub efficiently. A well-optimized partitioning strategy ensures parallel processing, balanced workload distribution, and cost efficiency, while a poor strategy leads to hot partitions, uneven throughput, and unnecessary compute/storage costs.Highlighted below are partitioning best practices, optimization techniques, and cost trade-offs.Understanding Partitioning In Event HubEach partition in Azure Event Hub functions as an independent, ordered log stream, meaning -✔ Events within a partition are processed sequentially.✔ Different partitions can be processed in parallel, improving throughput and scalability.✔ Events must be assigned to partitions based on a partition key.Partitioning Model ExampleBest Practices For PartitioningChoosing the right partitioning strategy is essential for balancing performance, scalability, and cost efficiency.1️⃣ Choose The Right Number Of Partitions Based On Message Volume & Parallelism NeedsMore partitions allow for greater parallel processing, but having too many can be wasteful and increase costs.Azure Event Hub partitions cannot be changed after creation — choose wisely upfront.Small workloads (~1000 events/sec) → 2–4 partitions.Medium workloads (~100K events/sec) → 6–12 partitions.Large-scale workloads (~1M+ events/sec) → 16+ partitions.Creating an Event Hub with Optimized Partition Countaz eventhubs eventhub create \ --name myeventhub \ --resource-group mygroup \ --namespace-name mynamespace \💡 Do not overprovision partitions — each partition introduces overhead in terms of storage, compute, and checkpointing.2️⃣ Use Consistent Partition Keys To Balance Message DistributionPartition keys determine how messages are distributed across partitions. A poor partitioning key can lead to hot partitions (overloaded partitions), while a good key ensures even workload distribution.Partition Key Best Practices✔ Use a high-cardinality key (e.g., customer ID, device ID, order ID).✔ Avoid using timestamps or sequential IDs as keys (leads to unbalanced partitions).✔ Ensure randomness in key distribution to prevent partition skew.Example: Assigning Partition Keys in Pythonfrom azure.eventhub import EventHubProducerClient, EventDataproducer = EventHubProducerClient.from_connection_string( "Endpoint=sb://myeventhubnamespace.servicebus.windows.net/", eventhub_name="my-event-hub")event_data_batch = producer.create_batch()event_data_batch.add(EventData("Order processed", partition_key="customer_123"))producer.send_batch(event_data_batch)💡 Choose partition keys carefully to avoid imbalanced partitions that degrade performance.3️⃣ Avoid Hot Partitions — Prevent Overloaded Partitions That Cause Processing DelaysA hot partition occurs when one partition receives a disproportionately high volume of events, leading to -✘ High processing latency for events in that partition.✘ Underutilization of other partitions, causing inefficiencies.✘ Increased storage costs due to excessive checkpointing.How To Prevent Hot Partitions✔ Monitor partition load using Azure Monitor.✔ Use a hashing function on partition keys to ensure even distribution.✔ If using IoT devices, distribute keys across multiple attributes (e.g., DeviceID + Location).Monitoring Partition Load in Azure CLIaz monitor metrics list \ --resource myeventhub \ --metric "IncomingMessages" \ --filter "PartitionId eq '0'"💡 Consistently check partition health — hot partitions signal poor key selection or suboptimal scaling.Cost Optimization With PartitioningPartitioning has direct cost implications — each partition requires -✔ Storage for events (log retention).✔ Compute resources for consumers (more partitions = more instances needed).✔ Additional checkpointing and transaction costs.1️⃣ Avoid Over-Provisioning PartitionsEach partition incurs Azure storage and compute overhead.More partitions = more checkpointing costs.Use the minimum partitions required for parallelism needs.Analyzing Partition Utilization to Reduce Unnecessary Costsaz monitor metrics list \ --resource myeventhub \ --metric "IncomingMessagesPerSecond"💡 If your partitions are underutilized, consider consolidating them to reduce cost overhead.2️⃣ Using Consumer Groups Efficiently To Maximize ThroughputConsumer groups allow multiple applications to process the same events independently, preventing the need to duplicate data ingestion.✔ Instead of creating multiple event hubs, use consumer groups to allow different teams/services to read the same event stream.✔ Each consumer group gets an independent view of the data, meaning different services can process events in parallel without affecting each other.✔ Avoid redundant consumer groups — each additional group introduces additional read operations, increasing costs.Creating a Consumer Group to Maximize Partition Utilizationaz eventhubs eventhub consumer-group create \ --name analyticsConsumer \ --eventhub-name myeventhub \ --namespace-name mynamespace \💡 Optimize consumer groups to share partitions efficiently, reducing the need for duplicated storage and compute costs.Final Thought — Optimizing Partitioning For Cost & PerformanceKey Takeaways For Partition Optimization✅ Don’t over-provision partitions — start small and scale as needed.✅ Use high-cardinality partition keys to ensure even distribution.✅ Monitor and prevent hot partitions to avoid performance bottlenecks.✅ Leverage consumer groups to maximize throughput without duplicating event streams.Scaling Event Hub Ingestion Without Over-ProvisioningScaling Azure Event Hub ingestion requires a careful balance between throughput, cost, and efficiency. Over-provisioning throughput units (TUs) or inefficient event ingestion patterns can result in excessive storage and compute costs.As part of this section, we go over best practices for optimizing producer costs, auto-scaling event ingestion, and reducing retention expenses using Event Hubs Capture.Event producers are the first point of optimization when managing high-volume event streams. Every unnecessary event, excessive API call, or unoptimized message format increases costs and processing overhead.1️⃣ Use Batching & Compression To Reduce Transaction CostsEach event sent to Event Hub incurs a storage transaction. If producers send individual events, this leads to millions of unnecessary transactions. Instead, batch multiple events together before sending them.✔ Batching minimizes storage transactions by sending a single payload containing multiple events.✔ Compression reduces data volume, lowering bandwidth and storage costs.✔ Batching improves ingestion efficiency by reducing API calls to Event Hub. Sending Batched Events Using Pythonfrom azure.eventhub import EventHubProducerClient, EventDataproducer = EventHubProducerClient.from_connection_string( "Endpoint=sb://myeventhubnamespace.servicebus.windows.net/", eventhub_name="my-event-hub") batch = producer.create_batch() for i in range(100): # Adding 100 events to the batch batch.add(EventData(f"Event {i}")) producer.send_batch(batch) # Send the batch as a single transaction💡 Batching 100 events per transaction reduces storage transactions by 99%, significantly lowering costs.2️⃣ Optimize Message Size To Fit Within The 256 KB Event Hub LimitAzure Event Hub charges based on event size — sending fragmented or oversized messages increases costs.✔ Keep messages under 256 KB to avoid fragmentation across multiple events.✔ Use efficient serialization (e.g., Avro, Protobuf) instead of JSON for large messages.✔ Apply Gzip compression before sending payloads.Compressing Events Before Sending to Event Hubimport gzipimport jsonfrom azure.eventhub import EventDatadata = {"sensor_id": 101, "temperature": 72.5, "status": "ok"}compressed_payload = gzip.compress(json.dumps(data).encode("utf-8"))event = EventData(compressed_payload)producer.send(event)💡 Compressed events reduce bandwidth and storage costs without affecting data integrity.3️⃣ Implement Efficient Partition Key Selection To Distribute Messages EvenlyPoor partitioning can result in hot partitions, leading to latency, throttling, and increased compute/storage costs.✔ Use a high-cardinality partition key (e.g., device_id, customer_id).✔ Avoid timestamps or sequential IDs as partition keys (creates uneven distribution).✔ Hash partition keys to ensure even message distribution across partitions.Assigning Partition Keys Dynamicallyevent_data = EventData("Sensor reading")event_data.partition_key = "device_123" # Ensuring even distributionproducer.send_batch([event_data])💡 Evenly distributed partitions prevent bottlenecks, improving throughput and reducing compute costs.Autoscaling Producers With Event HubsInstead of manually scaling event producers, leverage autoscaling mechanisms to dynamically handle traffic spikes and optimize TU consumption.1️⃣ Use Azure Functions With Event Hub Triggers For AutoscalingAzure Functions automatically scale based on incoming event volume, eliminating the need for manual provisioning of consumers.✔ Consumes events as they arrive without over-provisioning compute resources.✔ Scales up or down automatically based on event load.✔ Avoids unnecessary TU consumption during idle periods.Azure Function with Event Hub Triggerimport azure.functions as funcdef main(event: func.EventHubEvent): message = event.get_body().decode('utf-8') print(f"Processing event: {message}")💡 Using serverless processing eliminates the need for dedicated VMs or over-provisioned consumers.2️⃣ Implement Load-Balancing Mechanisms In ProducersIn high-throughput scenarios, producers can become a bottleneck. Using multiple producers with load balancing improves ingestion efficiency.✔ Use Kafka Connect or Azure IoT Edge modules for distributed ingestion.✔ Leverage a message queue (e.g., Azure Service Bus, RabbitMQ) to buffer event bursts.✔ Dynamically adjust producer instance counts based on incoming workload. Scaling Producers Dynamically Using KubernetesapiVersion: apps/v1kind: Deployment name: eventhub-producer replicas: 3 # Auto-scale producer pods spec: - name: producer image: my-eventhub-producer:latest💡 Auto-scaling producers prevents throttling and optimizes TU consumption.Using Event Hubs Capture For Cost SavingsInstead of retaining raw events in Event Hub for long periods, use Event Hubs Capture to offload data to Azure Blob Storage or Data Lake.1️⃣ Benefits Of Event Hubs Capture✔ Automatically offloads raw event data for long-term storage.✔ Reduces Event Hub retention costs (no need for extended data retention in expensive storage).✔ Enables downstream batch processing in Data Lake or Azure Synapse.2️⃣ How Event Hubs Capture WorksEvents are written in Avro format to Azure Blob Storage.Capture can be configured at the Event Hub level.Processed data can then be read using Databricks, Synapse Analytics, or Spark.Enabling Event Hubs Capture via Azure CLIaz eventhubs eventhub update \ --name myeventhub \ --resource-group mygroup \ --enable-capture true \ --destination-name myblobstorage \ --destination-container eventhub-logs💡 Instead of paying for extended retention in Event Hub, store raw events in Blob Storage at a fraction of the cost.Final Thought: Scaling Ingestion Efficiently Without Unnecessary CostsKey Takeaways For Efficient Event Hub Scaling✅ Batch and compress events to reduce storage costs.✅ Use consistent partitioning keys to ensure even message distribution.✅ Implement autoscaling with Azure Functions to handle event bursts efficiently.✅ Offload raw events using Event Hubs Capture to minimize retention costs.Scaling Event Processing Without Increasing CostsScaling event processing efficiently is as critical as optimizing ingestion. Without proper configuration, consumer inefficiencies — such as unbalanced processing, excessive checkpointing, and redundant parallel readers — can lead to increased storage costs, unnecessary compute usage, and performance bottlenecks.Below, we delve into consumer scaling, optimizing Azure Functions & Stream Analytics, and reducing checkpointing overhead to achieve cost-effective event processing.Efficient Consumer ScalingConsumers in Azure Event Hub read and process events in real time. Poor consumer management leads to -✘ Over-provisioned compute resources (unnecessary VMs, serverless instances).✘ High storage costs from excessive checkpointing.✘ Underutilized partitions, leading to inefficient parallelism.1️⃣ Use Azure Event Processor Host (EPH) Or Azure SDK-Based Processors For Balanced ScalingThe Azure Event Processor Host (EPH) and Azure Event Hub SDK-based processors automatically distribute processing workloads across partitions, ensuring optimal parallelism without over-scaling.✔ Dynamically assigns partitions to consumers, preventing overloading.✔ Handles partition failover automatically, ensuring resilience.✔ Minimizes idle consumers that contribute to unnecessary compute costs.Using the Azure Event Hub SDK for Balanced Processingfrom azure.eventhub import EventHubConsumerClientdef on_event(partition_context, event): print(f"Received event from partition: {partition_context.partition_id}") partition_context.update_checkpoint(event) # Checkpoint only periodicallyconsumer = EventHubConsumerClient.from_connection_string( "Endpoint=sb://myeventhubnamespace.servicebus.windows.net/", consumer_group="$Default", eventhub_name="my-event-hub" consumer.receive(on_event=on_event, starting_position="-1")💡 Let Event Processor Host (EPH) dynamically assign partitions instead of manually assigning partitions to consumers.2️⃣ Use Multiple Consumer Groups Strategically Instead Of Excessive Parallel ReadersConsumer groups allow different applications to independently process the same event stream. However, excessive consumer groups can drive up costs by increasing storage transactions and compute usage.Best Practices For Cost-Effective Consumer Groups✔ Use multiple consumer groups only when absolutely necessary (e.g., separate analytics, fraud detection, and real-time dashboards).✔ Avoid spinning up multiple consumer instances per partition — Azure automatically balances partitions across consumers.✔ Monitor consumer group activity to identify idle or underutilized consumers.Creating an Optimized Consumer Group in Azure CLIaz eventhubs eventhub consumer-group create \ --name analyticsGroup \ --eventhub-name myeventhub \ --namespace-name mynamespace \💡 One consumer per partition is optimal. Avoid redundant parallel readers to reduce compute and storage overhead.Optimizing Azure Functions & Stream Analytics For Cost-Effective Consumption1️⃣ Azure Functions Event Hub Trigger — Autoscale Based On Event LoadAzure Functions automatically scale based on event traffic from Event Hub, eliminating the need to pre-provision compute resources.✔ Pay only for function execution time — no idle resource costs.✔ Automatically scales consumers as event load increases.✔ Leverages Event Hub Checkpoints to ensure at-least-once processing. Azure Function with Event Hub Trigger (Python)import azure.functions as funcdef main(event: func.EventHubEvent): message = event.get_body().decode('utf-8') print(f"Processing event: {message}")💡 Using serverless functions prevents over-provisioning while dynamically scaling processing power.2️⃣ Azure Stream Analytics Query Optimization — Reduce Unnecessary Aggregations & FilteringAzure Stream Analytics processes real-time event streams, but inefficient queries can cause -✘ High CPU utilization and memory usage, increasing costs.✘ Unnecessary filtering operations, increasing query processing time.✘ Excessive state retention, driving up storage costs.Best Practices For Stream Analytics Query Optimization✔ Filter events as early as possible — avoid SELECT * queries.✔ Use tumbling windows instead of sliding windows to reduce state retention.✔ Optimize aggregations to reduce duplicate computations.Optimized Stream Analytics QuerySELECT deviceId, AVG(temperature) AS avg_tempFROM inputStreamGROUP BY deviceId, TumblingWindow(second, 60) -- More efficient than sliding window💡 Pre-filter and aggregate events efficiently to minimize unnecessary compute overhead.Using Checkpointing EfficientlyCheckpointing ensures that event processing resumes from the last processed event in case of failures. However, excessive checkpointing results in unnecessary Azure Storage transactions, leading to higher costs and reduced performance.1️⃣ Avoid Excessive Checkpointing To Reduce Storage & Performance OverheadBy default, each checkpoint writes state to Azure Storage, incurring transaction costs. Instead -✔ Checkpoint periodically (e.g., every 500 events) instead of per event.✔ Use in-memory state tracking to minimize unnecessary writes.✔ Reduce checkpoint frequency for high-throughput workloads.Efficient Checkpointing Strategy in Pythondef on_event(partition_context, event): print(f"Processing event from partition: {partition_context.partition_id}") # Only checkpoint after processing 500 events if event.sequence_number % 500 == 0: partition_context.update_checkpoint(event)💡 Checkpointing every event dramatically increases storage costs. Checkpoint only at intervals to optimize performance.2️⃣ Implement Intelligent Checkpointing Strategies (Periodic Instead Of Per-Event)The right checkpointing frequency depends on event volume, processing time, and failure tolerance.Reducing Checkpointing Frequency in Stream AnalyticsSELECT deviceId, COUNT(*) AS event_countINTO outputGROUP BY TumblingWindow(minute, 5) -- Checkpointing every 5 minutes💡 Larger checkpoint intervals reduce storage costs without compromising event recovery.Final Thought: Scaling Event Processing EfficientlyKey Takeaways For Cost-Efficient Event Processing✅ Balance consumer workloads with Azure Event Processor Host to prevent redundant processing.✅ Use multiple consumer groups only when necessary to minimize duplicated reads.✅ Auto-scale event processing with Azure Functions to reduce compute waste.✅ Optimize Stream Analytics queries to avoid unnecessary aggregations.✅ Reduce checkpointing frequency to minimize Azure Storage transaction costs.Cost-Effective Autoscaling StrategiesAutoscaling is a key optimization strategy for Azure Event Hub, ensuring that you only pay for what you use while maintaining high throughput and low latency. Without proper autoscaling, organizations often over-provision throughput units (TUs), leading to wasted compute resources and higher-than-necessary costs.In this section, we go over cost-efficient autoscaling strategies, including TU autoscaling, Event Hub Auto-Inflate, and Dedicated Clusters for large-scale workloads.Throughput Unit (TU) AutoscalingAzure Event Hub charges based on the number of Throughput Units (TUs) provisioned, regardless of whether they are fully utilized. Static TU allocation can lead to excessive costs when demand fluctuates.1️⃣ Scale TUs Dynamically Instead Of Over-Provisioning✔ Adjust TUs based on actual workload demand instead of keeping a fixed allocation.✔ Use Azure Monitor to track TU usage and trigger autoscaling based on ingestion rates.✔ Prevent unnecessary spending by scaling down during off-peak hours.Autoscaling TUs Using Azure CLIaz eventhubs namespace update \ --name mynamespace \ --resource-group mygroup \ --enable-auto-inflate \ --maximum-throughput-units 20Setting Up an Alert for High TU Utilization in Azure Monitoraz monitor metrics alert create \ --name "EventHubTUScaling" \ --resource-group mygroup \ --scopes /subscriptions/{subscription-id}/resourceGroups/mygroup/providers/Microsoft.EventHub/namespaces/mynamespace \ --condition "max total throughput units > 15" \ --action-group my-action-group💡 Autoscaling ensures that Event Hub scales up when needed but scales down when traffic reduces, avoiding over-provisioning costs.Azure Event Hub Auto-Inflate allows dynamic scaling of TUs while avoiding excessive provisioning costs. Instead of manually increasing TUs, Auto-Inflate expands capacity as needed while preventing runaway scaling.1️⃣ Configure Auto-Inflate To Prevent Throttling While Controlling Costs✔ Automatically increases TUs when event throughput spikes.✔ Prevents throttling while avoiding unnecessary scaling.✔ Ensures predictable cost control by setting a maximum TU limit.Best Practices For Configuring Auto-InflateSet a maximum TU cap to prevent cost overruns.Monitor usage trends to fine-tune auto-scaling thresholds.Avoid setting the max TU cap too high — match it to realistic peak loads.Enabling Auto-Inflate with a TU Cap of 10az eventhubs namespace update \ --name mynamespace \ --resource-group mygroup \ --enable-auto-inflate true \ --maximum-throughput-units 10💡 Auto-Inflate helps scale TUs dynamically, but a max cap ensures cost predictability.Using Dedicated Clusters For Cost Efficiency In Large DeploymentsFor enterprises processing hundreds of millions of events per day, Dedicated Event Hub Clusters provide cost savings at extreme scale. Instead of paying per TU, dedicated clusters offer a flat-rate pricing model with unlimited throughput.1️⃣ When To Choose Dedicated Event Hub Clusters✔ If your workload regularly exceeds 40+ TUs, Dedicated Clusters can reduce per-event costs.✔ Ideal for mission-critical, high-ingestion workloads like financial transactions, IoT telemetry, and fraud detection.✔ Fixed monthly pricing removes variability in costs.2️⃣ Comparing Costs — Standard Vs. Dedicated💡 For workloads exceeding 40+ TUs consistently, Dedicated Clusters are often the most cost-efficient option.Final Thought: Implementing Cost-Efficient Autoscaling StrategiesKey Takeaways For Cost-Optimized Autoscaling✅ Enable TU autoscaling and monitor usage patterns to prevent unnecessary provisioning.✅ Use Auto-Inflate to dynamically expand capacity while capping costs.✅ For massive workloads (100M+ events/day), consider Dedicated Event Hub Clusters.✅ Continuously monitor TU utilization in Azure Monitor to optimize scale settings.Monitoring & Optimization For Cost ControlOptimizing Azure Event Hub costs is not just about autoscaling and efficient processing — it requires continuous monitoring, anomaly detection, and proactive cost management. Without proper observability, organizations risk paying for underutilized resources, inefficient partitioning, and unnecessary storage transactions.Below, we deal with how to track Event Hub usage using Azure Monitor, detect inefficiencies in event processing, and leverage Azure Cost Management for budget control.Using Azure Monitor & Metrics For Cost ControlAzure Monitor provides real-time insights into Event Hub ingestion, processing, and throughput utilization. Tracking the right metrics helps avoid over-provisioning, optimize autoscaling, and detect inefficient workloads.1️⃣ Track Key Event Hub Metrics✔ Measures event ingestion rate to scale throughput efficiently.✔ Helps identify spikes in producer traffic and optimize batching.✔ Throughput Units (TUs) Usage: Tracks TU consumption to ensure autoscaling is optimized. Querying Event Hub Metrics via Azure CLIaz monitor metrics list \ --resource myeventhubnamespace \ --metric "IncomingMessages"2️⃣ Set Up Cost Alerts To Detect Inefficient ScalingAzure Monitor alerts can notify teams when TUs are underutilized or event processing is inefficient.✔ Detect sudden increases in TU usage — preventing unnecessary cost spikes.✔ Identify periods of low usage — suggesting opportunities for TU scaling down.✔ Alert on excessive storage transactions — indicating checkpointing inefficiencies.Creating a Cost Alert for TU Usage Exceeding 80%az monitor metrics alert create \ --name "TU_Usage_Alert" \ --resource-group mygroup \ --scopes /subscriptions/{subscription-id}/resourceGroups/mygroup/providers/Microsoft.EventHub/namespaces/mynamespace \ --condition "max total throughput units > 8" \ --action-group my-action-group💡 Proactively setting up alerts prevents runaway costs and ensures autoscaling is functioning as expected.Detecting Anomalies & Inefficiencies1️⃣ Identify Underutilized Partitions & Optimize Load BalancingPartition imbalances cause hot partitions (overloaded) and idle partitions (wasted resources). Azure Monitor can detect these inefficiencies and help rebalance partition loads.Best Practices For Partition Optimization✔ Track per-partition load metrics to detect imbalances.✔ Reconfigure partition keys if one partition is disproportionately high.✔ Use Event Processor Host (EPH) to dynamically balance consumers.Example: Monitoring Partition Load with Azure CLIaz monitor metrics list \ --resource myeventhub \ --metric "IncomingMessagesPerSecond" \ --filter "PartitionId eq '0'"💡 Balancing partitions reduces processing bottlenecks and improves throughput efficiency.2️⃣ Detect Long-Running Or Failed Consumers That May Cause Increased CostsFailed or slow consumers lead to backlogged events, forcing Event Hub to retain messages longer than necessary.✔ Monitor consumer lag and identify slow event processing.✔ Detect consumers that checkpoint too frequently, increasing storage costs.✔ Restart or scale up slow consumers to ensure real-time processing.Checking for Long-Running Event Hub Consumersaz monitor metrics list \ --resource myeventhub \ --metric "EventHubConsumerLag"💡 Detecting slow or failing consumers early prevents unnecessary storage and compute costs.Azure Cost Management IntegrationAzure Cost Management + Billing allows teams to track spending trends, set budgets, and optimize cost allocation for Event Hub.1️⃣ Track Event Hub Spending Trends✔ Analyze cost breakdown by TUs, storage transactions, and egress data.✔ Identify periods of high TU usage and adjust scaling policies.✔ Detect unoptimized workloads that lead to unnecessary spending.Viewing Event Hub Costs in Azure Cost Managementaz consumption usage list --query "[?contains(name, 'Event Hub')]"💡 Regularly reviewing cost trends helps identify inefficiencies before they become expensive.2️⃣ Set Budgets & Alerts For Cost Control✔ Define monthly budgets to ensure Event Hub costs remain within expected limits.✔ Automatically alert teams if spending exceeds thresholds.✔ Optimize TU allocation based on cost trends.Creating an Azure Cost Budget for Event Hubaz consumption budget create \ --name "EventHubBudget" \ --resource-group mygroup \ --amount 1000 \ --category Cost💡 Proactive budgeting prevents cost overruns and ensures resources are used efficiently.Final Thought: Optimizing Event Hub Costs Through Monitoring & AutomationKey Takeaways For Cost-Optimized Event Hub Monitoring✅ Track TU usage and event throughput to optimize autoscaling.✅ Set alerts to detect over-provisioning and prevent cost spikes.✅ Balance partitions to avoid bottlenecks and maximize efficiency.✅ Monitor consumer processing times to prevent excessive storage retention.✅ Leverage Azure Cost Management to enforce budget limits.Comparing Azure Event Hub Scaling With Other SolutionsAzure Event Hub is a high-throughput event ingestion service, but it is not the only event streaming solution available. Depending on scalability, latency, cost, and integration needs, enterprises may consider Apache Kafka, AWS Kinesis, or Google Pub/Sub as alternatives.In this section, we undertake a comparative analysis of Event Hub vs. Kafka, Kinesis, and Pub/Sub, as well as also highlight hybrid architecture strategies that combine Event Hub with Kafka and stream processing pipelines.Event Hub Vs. Apache Kafka On AzureAzure Event Hub and Apache Kafka serve similar functions but differ in architecture, scalability, and operational complexity.1️⃣ When To Use Azure Event Hub✔ Managed PaaS (Platform-as-a-Service) with zero infrastructure management.✔ Built-in Azure integrations (e.g., Azure Functions, Stream Analytics, Synapse, Data Lake).✔ Lower operational overhead — no need for ZooKeeper, brokers, or cluster management.✔ Throughput Unit (TU)-based scaling, auto-inflates on demand.💡 Cloud-native applications, serverless event processing, and real-time analytics in Azure.2️⃣ When To Use Azure Managed Kafka (Confluent Cloud, HDInsight Kafka, Or AKS Kafka)✔ Requires fine-tuned control over partitions, offsets, and replication factors.✔ Supports advanced stream processing (Kafka Streams, KSQL, Flink).✔ Offers cross-region, multi-cloud event streaming with custom retention policies.✔ Can be deployed on AKS (Azure Kubernetes Service) for self-managed scaling.💡 Complex event processing pipelines with cross-cloud streaming requirements.3️⃣ Key Differences — Event Hub Vs. Apache Kafka On Azure💡 Use Event Hub for serverless event streaming and Kafka for advanced stream processing & cross-cloud architectures.Event Hub Vs. AWS Kinesis & Google Pub/SubWhen choosing between Azure Event Hub, AWS Kinesis, and Google Pub/Sub, the key considerations are latency, cost, scalability, and cloud-native integrations.1️⃣ Latency & Performance Comparison💡 Azure Event Hub offers lower latency than Kinesis and Pub/Sub while scaling dynamically without manual sharding.2️⃣ Cost Comparison — Event Hub Vs. Kinesis Vs. Pub/Sub💡 Event Hub offers more predictable pricing than Kinesis (which charges per record) and Google Pub/Sub (which charges per TB).Hybrid Architectures — Using Event Hub With Kafka & Stream Processing PipelinesIn some cases, Event Hub and Kafka can be used together to build scalable, cross-cloud, and hybrid event streaming architectures.1️⃣ Using Event Hub As A Kafka-Compatible Ingestion Layer✔ Event Hub supports Kafka API, meaning Kafka applications can send/receive events without modifications.✔ Enables cloud-native Kafka streaming without managing Kafka infrastructure.✔ Ideal for organizations migrating from on-prem Kafka to Azure. Kafka Producer Writing to Azure Event Hubfrom kafka import KafkaProducerproducer = KafkaProducer( bootstrap_servers="myeventhubnamespace.servicebus.windows.net:9093", security_protocol="SASL_SSL", sasl_mechanism="PLAIN", sasl_plain_username="$ConnectionString", sasl_plain_password="Endpoint=sb://myeventhubnamespace..."producer.send("my-eventhub", b"Kafka message to Event Hub")💡 Use Event Hub as a managed Kafka alternative for cloud-native event streaming.2️⃣ Integrating Event Hub With Stream Processing Pipelines✔ Combine Event Hub with Apache Spark, Azure Stream Analytics, or Flink for real-time analytics.✔ Use Event Hub Capture to offload raw events to Azure Data Lake for long-term storage.✔ Enable multi-cloud event processing by streaming events into Kafka, Snowflake, or AWS Lambda.Stream Processing Pipeline with Event Hub & Azure Stream AnalyticsSELECT deviceId, COUNT(*) AS event_countINTO outputGROUP BY deviceId, TumblingWindow(minute, 5)💡 Event Hub integrates seamlessly with cloud-native stream processing for real-time analytics.Final Thought: Choosing the Right Event Streaming SolutionKey Takeaways For Comparing Event Hub With Other Solutions✅ Use Event Hub for serverless event streaming in Azure.✅ Use Managed Kafka when advanced stream processing (Flink, KSQL) is needed.✅ Event Hub offers lower latency than Kinesis and Pub/Sub with auto-scaling TUs.✅ Integrate Event Hub with Kafka or Stream Analytics for hybrid architectures.Future Trends In Scaling Azure Event HubAs real-time event streaming continues to evolve, the need for smarter, cost-efficient, and AI-driven scaling is becoming more critical. Future advancements in predictive autoscaling, serverless event streaming, and seamless integration with analytics platforms will shape the next generation of Azure Event Hub scaling strategies.Below, we explore AI-driven autoscaling, serverless event processing innovations, and cost-effective analytics integrations with Azure Synapse & Databricks.Event Processing with AI & Predictive AutoscalingTraditional Throughput Unit (TU) scaling is reactive, adjusting based on current demand. Future innovations will leverage AI-based predictive autoscaling to forecast event traffic and scale resources proactively.1️⃣ AI-Driven Traffic Predictions For Dynamic Scaling✔ Machine learning models analyze historical event patterns to predict spikes and scale resources before congestion occurs.✔ Avoids sudden cost spikes by gradually increasing TUs instead of over-provisioning.✔ Reduces throttling risks during unpredictable traffic surges.AI-Driven Autoscaling with Azure Machine Learningfrom azure.ai.ml import MLClientfrom azure.ai.ml.entities import Job# Train AI model on historical Event Hub traffic datajob = Job( compute="azureml-cluster", command="python train_traffic_model.py", inputs={"eventhub_data": "azure_blob_storage"})ml_client.jobs.create_or_update(job)💡 Predictive autoscaling allows Event Hub to scale resources in anticipation of traffic surges, reducing unexpected costs and performance degradation.2️⃣ Intelligent Autoscaling With Reinforcement Learning (RL)✔ Reinforcement learning models dynamically optimize TU scaling strategies based on real-time feedback loops.✔ RL-based policies continuously improve scaling decisions, reducing manual intervention.✔ Enables self-optimizing Event Hub deployments with minimal cost overhead.Reinforcement Learning Model for Autoscalingfrom azureml.core import Environment, ScriptRunConfigenv = Environment.from_pip_requirements(name="rl-env", file_path="requirements.txt")config = ScriptRunConfig(source_directory="./rl_training", script="train.py", environment=env)ml_client.jobs.create_or_update(config)💡 AI-driven scaling removes inefficiencies in manual autoscaling, leading to reduced operational costs and better performance.Serverless Event Streaming InnovationsThe future of event streaming will move further toward serverless architectures, eliminating manual scaling complexities.1️⃣ Fully Managed Serverless Event Streaming✔ Next-gen Event Hub services will automatically manage partitions, scaling, and load balancing without manual intervention.✔ Serverless processing will integrate deeply with Azure Functions & Logic Apps, allowing low-latency event handling.✔ Pricing models will shift toward per-event-based billing instead of TU-based fixed allocations.Event Hub Serverless Trigger with Azure Logic Apps{ "type": "Microsoft.EventHub/EventHubs", "properties": { "triggerType": "EventTrigger", "function": "ProcessEventHubData"}💡 Serverless event processing eliminates provisioning concerns, optimizing cost efficiency for dynamic workloads.2️⃣ AI-Optimized Event Routing & Processing✔ Event Hub will leverage AI models to dynamically route events to the most cost-efficient processing resources (e.g., Azure Functions, Kubernetes, or Databricks).✔ Intelligent event filtering at the ingestion level to reduce unnecessary event processing and storage costs.✔ Smart event deduplication mechanisms will reduce redundant events before reaching consumers.💡 Serverless event streaming + AI-based event routing will minimize cloud costs while maximizing processing efficiency.Integration With Azure Synapse & Databricks For Cost-Effective Real-Time AnalyticsAs real-time analytics grows, tight integration between Event Hub, Azure Synapse Analytics, and Databricks will be crucial for cost-efficient, large-scale event processing.1️⃣ Cost-Optimized Real-Time Analytics Pipelines✔ Direct ingestion from Event Hub to Azure Synapse Streaming Analytics, enabling SQL-based real-time analytics.✔ Databricks Delta Lake integration for high-throughput, low-latency processing.✔ Near real-time dashboarding in Power BI with reduced data processing lag.Streaming Data from Event Hub to Synapse AnalyticsCREATE EXTERNAL TABLE eventhub_data ( event_time datetime,) LOCATION = 'https://storageaccount.blob.core.windows.net/eventhub-logs', DATA_SOURCE = synapse_eventhub_source, FILE_FORMAT = ParquetFormat💡 Using Event Hub with Azure Synapse enables cost-efficient real-time analytics without expensive compute resources.2️⃣ Unified Event Processing With Databricks & Spark Streaming✔ Databricks’ Structured Streaming enables auto-scaling Spark clusters based on Event Hub traffic.✔ Delta Lake ensures high-performance analytics with ACID-compliant data ingestion.✔ Combines batch + streaming workloads for unified data processing.Processing Event Hub Data in Databricks with Spark Streamingfrom pyspark.sql.functions import * "eventhubs.connectionString": "<eventhub-connection-string>"df = spark.readStream.format("eventhubs").options(**eventHubConfig).load()df.writeStream.format("delta").outputMode("append").start("/mnt/datalake/eventhub")💡 Using Databricks for event processing scales analytics efficiently without over-provisioning resources.Final Thought: The Future of Azure Event Hub ScalingKey Takeaways For The Future Of Event Hub Scaling✅ AI-powered predictive autoscaling will replace manual TU provisioning.✅ Serverless event streaming will become the standard for cloud-native event processing.✅ Intelligent event routing will reduce redundant event processing costs.✅ Event Hub + Azure Synapse + Databricks will provide cost-efficient, real-time analytics pipelines.Scaling Azure Event Hub efficiently isn’t only about adding more throughput units (TUs) — it necessitates a well-architected balance between ingestion, processing, autoscaling, and cost optimization. Through the implementation of these best practices in partitioning, checkpointing, monitoring, and AI-driven scaling, our Azure specialists at Xinthe assist our enterprise clientele in building highly efficient, cost-aware event-driven architectures. Now, implementing these strategies, can help you too leverage Azure Event Hub as a scalable, cost-efficient event streaming platform while minimizing unnecessary resource consumption and operational expenses.

item.title: ChatLoopBackOff Episode 48 (Kubewarden)
item.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
item.content:encoded:
Kubewarden, a CNCF Sandbox project, is a policy engine for Kubernetes that allows administrators to define, enforce, and manage custom security and operational policies using WebAssembly (WASM). Policies can be written in any language that compiles to WASM, offering developers flexibility beyond traditional policy engines that require specific languages like Rego. Kubewarden policies are applied at admission control or runtime to ensure compliance and security. It is lightweight, fast, with a portable design, ensures minimal performance overhead and scalability in cloud-native environments.
Join CNCF Ambassador Carlos Santana as they explore its focus on extensibility, ease of use, and ecosystem compatibility, and how Kubewarden is ideal for organizations seeking fine-grained control over their Kubernetes clusters while simplifying policy authoring and enforcement.

item.title: NFTables mode for kube-proxy
item.content:encoded: A new nftables mode for kube-proxy was introduced as an alpha feature in Kubernetes 1.29. Currently in beta, it is expected to be GA as of 1.33. The new mode fixes long-standing performance problems with the iptables mode and all users running on systems with reasonably-recent kernels are encouraged to try it out. (For compatibility reasons, even once nftables becomes GA, iptables will still be the .)Why nftables? Part 1: data plane latencyThe iptables API was designed for implementing simple firewalls, and has problems scaling up to support Service proxying in a large Kubernetes cluster with tens of thousands of Services.In general, the ruleset generated by kube-proxy in iptables mode has a number of iptables rules proportional to the sum of the number of Services and the total number of endpoints. In particular, at the top level of the ruleset, there is one rule to test each possible Service IP (and port) that a packet might be addressed to:# If the packet is addressed to 172.30.0.41:80, then jump to the chain # KUBE-SVC-XPGD46QRK7WJZT7O for further processing -A KUBE-SERVICES -m comment --comment "namespace1/service1:p80 cluster IP" -m tcp -p tcp -d 172.30.0.41 --dport 80 -j KUBE-SVC-XPGD46QRK7WJZT7O # If the packet is addressed to 172.30.0.42:443, then... -A KUBE-SERVICES -m comment --comment "namespace2/service2:p443 cluster IP" -m tcp -p tcp -d 172.30.0.42 --dport 443 -j KUBE-SVC-GNZBNJ2PO5MGZ6GT # etc... -A KUBE-SERVICES -m comment --comment "namespace3/service3:p80 cluster IP" -m tcp -p tcp -d 172.30.0.43 --dport 80 -j KUBE-SVC-X27LE4BHSL4DOUIK This means that when a packet comes in, the time it takes the kernel to check it against all of the Service rules is in the number of Services. As the number of Services increases, both the average and the worst-case latency for the first packet of a new connection increases (with the difference between best-case, average, and worst-case being mostly determined by whether a given Service IP address appears earlier or later in the chain).By contrast, with nftables, the normal way to write a ruleset like this is to have a rule, using a "verdict map" to do the dispatch:table ip kube-proxy { # The service-ips verdict map indicates the action to take for each matching packet. map service-ips { type ipv4_addr . inet_proto . inet_service : verdict comment "ClusterIP, ExternalIP and LoadBalancer IP traffic" elements = { 172.30.0.41 . tcp . 80 : goto service-ULMVA6XW-namespace1/service1/tcp/p80, 172.30.0.42 . tcp . 443 : goto service-42NFTM6N-namespace2/service2/tcp/p443, 172.30.0.43 . tcp . 80 : goto service-4AT6LBPK-namespace3/service3/tcp/p80, ... } } # Now we just need a single rule to process all packets matching an # element in the map. (This rule says, "construct a tuple from the # destination IP address, layer 4 protocol, and destination port; look # that tuple up in "service-ips"; and if there's a match, execute the # associated verdict.) chain services { ip daddr . meta l4proto . th dport vmap @service-ips } ... } Since there's only a single rule, with a roughly map lookup, packet processing time is more or less constant regardless of cluster size, and the best/average/worst cases are very similar:But note the huge difference in the vertical scale between the iptables and nftables graphs! In the clusters with 5000 and 10,000 Services, the p50 (average) latency for nftables is about the same as the p01 (approximately best-case) latency for iptables. In the 30,000 Service cluster, the p99 (approximately worst-case) latency for nftables manages to beat out the p01 latency for iptables by a few microseconds! Here's both sets of data together, but you may have to squint to see the nftables results!:Why nftables? Part 2: control plane latencyWhile the improvements to data plane latency in large clusters are great, there's another problem with iptables kube-proxy that often keeps users from even being able to grow their clusters to that size: the time it takes kube-proxy to program new iptables rules when Services and their endpoints change.With both iptables and nftables, the total size of the ruleset as a whole (actual rules, plus associated data) is in the combined number of Services and their endpoints. Originally, the iptables backend would rewrite every rule on every update, and with tens of thousands of Services, this could grow to be hundreds of thousands of iptables rules. Starting in Kubernetes 1.26, we began improving kube-proxy so that it could skip updating of the unchanged rules in each update, but the limitations of as an API meant that it was still always necessary to send an update that's in the number of Services (though with a noticeably smaller constant than it used to be). Even with those optimizations, it can still be necessary to make use of kube-proxy's config option to ensure that it doesn't spend every waking second trying to push iptables updates.The nftables APIs allow for doing much more incremental updates, and when kube-proxy in nftables mode does an update, the size of the update is only in the number of Services and endpoints that have changed since the last sync, regardless of the total number of Services and endpoints. The fact that the nftables API allows each nftables-using component to have its own private table also means that there is no global lock contention between components like with iptables. As a result, kube-proxy's nftables updates can be done much more efficiently than with iptables.(Unfortunately I don't have cool graphs for this part.)All that said, there are a few reasons why you might not want to jump right into using the nftables backend for now.First, the code is still fairly new. While it has plenty of unit tests, performs correctly in our CI system, and has now been used in the real world by multiple users, it has not seen anything close to as much real-world usage as the iptables backend has, so we can't promise that it is as stable and bug-free.Second, the nftables mode will not work on older Linux distributions; currently it requires a 5.13 or newer kernel. Additionally, because of bugs in early versions of the command line tool, you should not run kube-proxy in nftables mode on nodes that have an old (earlier than 1.0.0) version of in the host filesystem (or else kube-proxy's use of nftables may interfere with other uses of nftables on the system).Third, you may have other networking components in your cluster, such as the pod network or NetworkPolicy implementation, that do not yet support kube-proxy in nftables mode. You should consult the documentation (or forums, bug tracker, etc.) for any such components to see if they have problems with nftables mode. (In many cases they will not; as long as they don't try to directly interact with or override kube-proxy's iptables rules, they shouldn't care whether kube-proxy is using iptables or nftables.) Additionally, observability and monitoring tools that have not been updated may report less data for kube-proxy in nftables mode than they do for kube-proxy in iptables mode.Finally, kube-proxy in nftables mode is intentionally not 100% compatible with kube-proxy in iptables mode. There are a few old kube-proxy features whose default behaviors are less secure, less performant, or less intuitive than we'd like, but where we felt that changing the default would be a compatibility break. Since the nftables mode is opt-in, this gave us a chance to fix those bad defaults without breaking users who weren't expecting changes. (In particular, with nftables mode, NodePort Services are now only reachable on their nodes' default IPs, as opposed to being reachable on all IPs, including , with iptables mode.) The kube-proxy documentation has more information about this, including information about metrics you can look at to determine if you are relying on any of the changed functionality, and what configuration options are available to get more backward-compatible behavior.Ready to try it out? In Kubernetes 1.31 and later, you just need to pass to kube-proxy (or set in your kube-proxy config file).You can also convert existing clusters from iptables (or ipvs) mode to nftables by updating the kube-proxy configuration and restarting the kube-proxy pods. (You do not need to reboot the nodes: when restarting in nftables mode, kube-proxy will delete any existing iptables or ipvs rules, and likewise, if you later revert back to iptables or ipvs mode, it will delete any existing nftables rules.)As mentioned above, while nftables is now the kube-proxy mode, it is not the , and we do not yet have a plan for changing that. We will continue to support the iptables mode for a long time.The future of the IPVS mode of kube-proxy is less certain: its main advantage over iptables was that it was faster, but certain aspects of the IPVS architecture and APIs were awkward for kube-proxy's purposes (for example, the fact that the device needs to have Service IP address assigned to it), and some parts of Kubernetes Service proxying semantics were difficult to implement using IPVS (particularly the fact that some Services had to have different endpoints depending on whether you connected to them from a local or remote client). And now, the nftables mode has the same performance as IPVS mode (actually, slightly better), without any of the downsides:(In theory the IPVS mode also has the advantage of being able to use various other IPVS functionality, like alternative "schedulers" for balancing endpoints. In practice, this ended up not being very useful, because kube-proxy runs independently on every node, and the IPVS schedulers on each node had no way of sharing their state with the proxies on other nodes, thus thwarting the effort to balance traffic more cleverly.)While the Kubernetes project does not have an immediate plan to drop the IPVS backend, it is probably doomed in the long run, and people who are currently using IPVS mode should try out the nftables mode instead (and file bugs if you think there is missing functionality in nftables mode that you can't work around).

item.title: AWS Chatbot is now named Amazon Q Developer
item.author: Aaron Sempf
item.content:encoded: Today, we’re excited to announce that AWS Chatbot has been renamed to Amazon Q Developer, representing an enhancement to developer productivity through generative AI-powered capabilities.This update represents more than a name change – it’s an enhancement of our chat-based DevOps capabilities. By combining AWS Chatbot’s proven functionality with Amazon Q’s generative AI capabilities, we’re providing developers with more intuitive, efficient tools for cloud resource management.Transition for Existing UsersThe transition to Amazon Q Developer maintains compatibility with most workflows. Current AWS Chatbot users should experience no disruption to their configurations, permissions, or established processes, except for the following use cases.Notifications: If you are using Q in chat applications to send notifications, then you don’t need to make any changes. Your notifications will start showing “Amazon Q” as the sender.Manual commands: The visible change is the new “@Amazon Q” command replacing the previous “@aws” mention in chat channels. If you are running commands manually, then you will use “@Amazon Q” instead of “@aws”.Tip: it is faster to type @Q. The chat platform displays auto complete recommendations with the matching app in the channel.We’ve maintained the original AWS ChatBot accessibility by offering Amazon Q Developer’s chat features through the Free tier. This ensures that teams of all sizes can benefit from these enhanced capabilities without additional costs. The renamed service is accessible in all commercial regions, maintaining the same geographical reach as the original AWS Chatbot service.Security remains paramount with Amazon Q Developer. The service maintains all existing security controls, including AWS Organizations Service Control Policies and chat application policies. Organizations can precisely control access to resources and features through granular IAM permissions and channel-specific guardrails. To take advantage of generative AI capabilities organizations will need to add to the configuration of their channel permissions.Enhanced Chat Capabilities for DevOpsAmazon Q Developer integration with Microsoft Teams and Slack transforms these chat applications into powerful DevOps command centers, where team members can monitor, diagnose, and optimize their AWS resources and applications. Amazon Q Developer in chat applications provide real-time visibility into environment states, helping team members quickly identify which resources are operational or experiencing issues.Team members can reduce incident response times and monitor performance issues, traffic spikes, infrastructure events, and security threats through DevOps tooling that enables custom notifications with team member tagging for critical application events, interactive action buttons and aliases for telemetry retrieval, and command execution in chat channels.Amazon Q in Slack channelBuilding on existing features like custom notifications and actions, command aliases, and Amazon Bedrock Agents integration, Amazon Q Developer uses natural language processing to understand context and intent. For example, when investigating resources in a region, you can ask, “What EC2 instances are in us-east-1?”. This natural language understanding streamlines interactions and improves efficiency.Ask Amazon Q about resources in AWS AccountAmazon Q Developer can be used for more comprehensive resource management and status monitoring in chat channels. It can be used to send alerts to chat channels on Amazon CloudWatch metrics for monitoring, or can be used to explore resources across regions or within an account. DevOps teams can execute queries, such as count all VPCs in a region, listing all subnets in a VPC or providing all details for Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (EC2) instances in a region, such as “provide all details for EC2 instances in us-east-1”, providing better visibility into infrastructure.Use Amazon Q to query AWS resources informationSetting up Amazon Q Developer involves a straightforward process through the Amazon Q Developer console or the AWS SDK. To interact with Amazon Q Developer’s generative AI capabilities, start by adding appropriate managed policies (AmazonQDeveloperAccess or AmazonQFullAccess) to your IAM roles. Your teams can then customize their notification preferences, set up automated responses, and configure security guardrails according to their specific requirements.We’re excited to see how you and your teams leverage these enhanced capabilities to streamline their DevOps workflows and improve collaboration.Aaron Sempf is Next Gen Tech Lead for the AWS Partner Organization in Asia-Pacific and Japan. With over twenty years in distributed system engineering design and development, he focuses on solving for large scale complex integration and event driven systems. In his spare time, he can be found coding prototypes for autonomous robots, IoT devices, distributed solutions and designing Agentic Architecture patterns for GenAI assisted business automation.

item.title: Legit Security Extends ASPM Platform to Provide More Vulnerability Context

item.title: Databricks Notebooks Vs. Python Scripts — Performance & Best Practices
item.author: Noel
item.content:encoded: Databricks Notebooks Vs. Python Scripts — Performance & Best PracticesThe choice between executing code in Databricks notebooks and running standalone Python scripts significantly impacts performance, memory management, and scalability.This article explores the fundamental differences between Python scripts and Databricks notebooks, how execution contexts impact memory and resource usage, and the best practices our Databricks specialists at Xinthe employ for optimizing workload execution in large-scale environments.Understanding Execution Modes In DatabricksDatabricks provides multiple ways to execute workloads, each with distinct execution behaviors, resource management strategies, and performance implications. The two primary execution modes are -, which run as standalone jobs and interact with Databricks programmatically., which provide an interactive, cell-based execution environment with built-in state management.Understanding how these execution modes interact with Databricks clusters, execution contexts, and job scheduling is critical for optimizing workload performance and memory efficiency.What Are Python Scripts In Databricks?1. Running .py Scripts As Standalone Jobs In DatabricksPython scripts in Databricks are typically executed as standalone jobs rather than through an interactive UI. These scripts run in non-interactive, batch processing mode, which means -The script starts execution immediately upon submission.Once execution is complete, the runtime process exits, freeing up memory.There is no persistent execution state between runs.Python scripts are commonly used for production workflows, ETL pipelines, and scheduled jobs that require repeatable execution without manual intervention.To submit a Python script as a job in Databricks -databricks jobs run-now --job-id <job-id>Alternatively, using REST API -curl -X POST https://<databricks-instance>/api/2.1/jobs/run-now \ -H "Authorization: Bearer <token>" \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{ "job_id": 1234 }'2. How Python Scripts Interact With Databricks Runtime & ClustersWhen a Python script runs in Databricks, it -Uses a dedicated execution context that is terminated upon script completion.Loads libraries and dependencies specified in the job configuration.Can be scheduled and managed through .Executes as a single process within a cluster node, without interactive state persistence.Unlike notebooks, Python scripts do not retain execution state across multiple runs. This means that variables, imported libraries, and cached data do not persist once the script finishes execution.3. The Role Of databricks-cli & dbutils In Executing ScriptsPython scripts in Databricks can interact with the platform using -a) databricks-cli (Command Line Interface)Enables programmatic execution of scripts and job submission.Allows interaction with workflows, clusters, and job scheduling.Useful for integrating Databricks with CI/CD pipelines.Example: Running a Python script on Databricks from the CLI -databricks workspace import example.py /Workspace/Scripts/example.pydatabricks jobs create --json '{ "name": "PythonScriptJob", "new_cluster": { "spark_version": "12.0.x-scala2.12", "node_type_id": "Standard_DS3_v2" }, "spark_python_task": { "python_file": "/Workspace/Scripts/example.py" }}'b) dbutils (Databricks Utilities API)Provides helper functions for interacting with Databricks clusters, filesystems, secrets, and widgets.Can be called within a Python script using dbutils.fs, dbutils.secrets, and dbutils.jobs.Example: Using dbutils to interact with Databricks FileStoredbutils.fs.put("/mnt/mybucket/test.txt", "This is a test file", overwrite=True)What Are Databricks Notebooks?Unlike standalone scripts, Databricks notebooks provide an interactive execution environment that retains execution state across multiple runs.1. Execution Within The Databricks UINotebooks allow users to -Execute code in an interactive cell-based format.Retain session state, meaning variables, imports, and cached data persist across different runs.Mix different languages (Python, Scala, SQL, R) in a single workflow.Visualize data inline using built-in graphing and display functions.Example: A simple stateful execution in a notebook -counter = 0 # Declaring a variable in one cellcounter += 1 # Running this cell will update the value, persisting stateprint(counter)In Python scripts, this state would not persist across runs, whereas in notebooks, the value of counter carries over across multiple executions.2. Magic Commands (%python, %sql, %scala, %md) & Cell-Based ExecutionDatabricks notebooks provide built-in magic commands that allow execution of different languages within the same notebook.Example: Running SQL within a Python notebook -%sqlSELECT * FROM sales_data LIMIT 10;This allows seamless integration of data exploration and transformations without switching environments.3. How Notebooks Manage Stateful Execution & Interactive WorkflowsOne of the key advantages of notebooks is state persistence, which enables -Retaining variable values across different cells.Caching results for reuse without re-executing queries.Maintaining library imports without needing to reload them in every execution.However, this also introduces performance challenges, including -Memory accumulation over time, leading to higher cluster resource consumption.Execution context retention, which may degrade performance if too many contexts are active simultaneously.Example: Potential memory leakage in notebooksimport pandas as pd# Loading a large dataset into memorydf = pd.read_csv("/mnt/data/large_file.csv")# The dataframe persists across multiple cells unless explicitly clearedIf this dataframe is not deleted, it remains in memory even if unused, potentially leading to out-of-memory (OOM) errors when running multiple notebooks on the same cluster.Key Differences Between Python Scripts & Databricks NotebooksUnderstanding execution contexts in Databricks is crucial for choosing the right execution mode.Python scripts provide lightweight, repeatable, and scalable execution but lack interactive features.Databricks notebooks enable rapid experimentation but come with higher memory consumption and execution overhead.Performance Considerations In Databricks ExecutionThe execution mode chosen in Databricks — Python scripts or Databricks notebooks — has significant implications for execution speed, parallelism, state management, and resource efficiency. Each approach has trade-offs that impact cluster utilization, job completion time, and cost efficiency.1. Startup Time Comparison — Notebooks Vs. Standalone Python ScriptsThe initial execution time of a Databricks workload depends on how the environment is initialized. Notebooks retain execution state across multiple runs, but they incur additional UI-layer overheads, making them slower to start than standalone Python scripts.2. UI Overheads In NotebooksDatabricks notebooks introduce UI-specific performance overheads, such as -Cell-by-cell execution dependencies, which slow down overall script execution.Execution history tracking, which consumes additional memory.Graphical rendering overhead for inline visualizations (e.g., using display() in Spark).These factors increase execution time, especially for long-running batch jobs that do not require UI interactions.3. How Python Scripts Bypass UI OverheadsPython scripts in Databricks execute directly on clusters without any UI-layer dependencies, leading to faster execution for batch workloads.Example: Running a Python script as a standalone job bypasses UI overhead -databricks jobs run-now --job-id <job-id>This eliminates the need to manually run cells and interact with the UI, making batch executions more efficient.Parallelism & Distributed ExecutionDatabricks workloads are designed to take advantage of distributed computing through Spark clusters, multiprocessing, and parallel execution patterns. However, the way parallelism is handled differs between notebooks and Python scripts.1. Parallel Execution In NotebooksNotebooks support limited parallel execution, relying on -Multi-threaded execution using %run to call other notebooks in parallel.Manual parallel execution of multiple notebooks using separate execution contexts.Cell-by-cell execution, which prevents full-scale parallelization.Example: Running multiple notebooks in parallel using %run -dbutils.notebook.run("/Workspace/Notebook_1", timeout_seconds=0)dbutils.notebook.run("/Workspace/Notebook_2", timeout_seconds=0)Limitations Of Notebook-Based Parallelism:Cannot fully utilize Spark’s distributed execution capabilities.Execution remains tied to interactive sessions, limiting efficiency.2. Parallel Execution In Python ScriptsPython scripts allow for full parallelism using -Multiprocessing (multiprocessing module) to run concurrent processes.Thread-based concurrency (concurrent.futures) for parallel I/O operations.Apache Spark’s distributed execution model, allowing scalable data processing.Example: Using Python’s multiprocessing module for parallel execution -from multiprocessing import Pool # Process a subset of datadata_chunks = [[1,2,3], [4,5,6], [7,8,9]] results = p.map(process_data, data_chunks)This distributes workload execution across multiple processes, significantly reducing execution time compared to notebooks.3. Batch Job Execution In Python ScriptsPython scripts can be scheduled as batch jobs using Databricks Job Workflows, ensuring optimal cluster utilization.Example: Running a batch Python script on Databricks -{ "name": "BatchJob", "spark_version": "12.0.x-scala2.12", "node_type_id": "Standard_DS3_v2" }, "python_file": "dbfs:/scripts/batch_process.py"}By leveraging Spark’s parallel execution, Python scripts maximize computational efficiency, reducing runtime significantly compared to notebooks.Checkpointing & Stateful ExecutionThe ability to retain execution state is a key differentiator between notebooks and Python scripts.1. Notebooks Retain Execution State Across CellsDatabricks notebooks allow stateful execution, meaning -✔ Variables persist across multiple cells.✔ Imported libraries do not need to be reloaded each time.✔ Intermediate results are available for debugging and re-use.Example: Retaining state in a notebook -# Cell 1: Define a variablecounter = 0# Cell 2: Modify the variablecounter += 1print(counter) # Output: 1However, persistent execution state can lead to unintended consequences -Memory accumulation over time, leading to higher cluster resource consumption.Difficulty in reproducing results if prior cell execution is not controlled.2. Python Scripts Must Explicitly Handle State PersistencePython scripts operate in a stateless manner, meaning that -All variables and execution states are lost after script completion.State persistence must be explicitly managed using checkpointing mechanisms (e.g., writing to storage).Example: Explicit state persistence using checkpointing -import picklewith open('/dbfs/tmp/state.pkl', 'wb') as f:with open('/dbfs/tmp/state.pkl', 'rb') as f:This ensures that state is persisted across script executions, unlike in notebooks.Cluster resource utilization is a key concern in Databricks performance optimization.1. Persistent Resource Allocation In NotebooksNotebooks retain execution contexts, keeping clusters allocated even when idle.Implications of persistent execution contexts -High memory usage if multiple notebooks are attached to the same cluster.Unused compute resources remain allocated, increasing Databricks infrastructure costs.Example: Checking active execution contexts in Databricks -print(spark.sparkContext.getExecutorMemoryStatus())This displays memory consumption across active execution contexts, which persists even if notebooks are inactive.2. Ephemeral Execution Of Python ScriptsPython scripts use ephemeral execution, meaning -Compute resources are allocated only when the script runs.Clusters spin up and shut down automatically, reducing idle costs.Example: Configuring an auto-termination policy for script execution -{ "new_cluster": { "spark_version": "12.0.x-scala2.12", "node_type_id": "Standard_DS3_v2", "autotermination_minutes": 15 }This ensures that clusters terminate after execution, optimizing cost efficiency.Choosing between Databricks notebooks and Python scripts requires evaluating -✔ — Python scripts execute faster by avoiding UI-layer overheads.✔ — Python scripts leverage multi-threading, multiprocessing, and Spark-based parallel execution.✔ — Notebooks retain execution context, while Python scripts require explicit checkpointing.✔ — Notebooks keep clusters allocated, whereas Python scripts run in ephemeral job mode, reducing costs.Development & Maintainability Trade-offs In DatabricksChoosing between Python scripts and Databricks notebooks is not just a matter of execution efficiency — it also impacts code maintainability, debugging workflows, and integration with CI/CD pipelines. While notebooks provide an intuitive, interactive environment, Python scripts enable better modularity, reusability, and adherence to software engineering best practices.This section explores the development trade-offs in terms of code modularity, debugging, logging, and version control strategies for large-scale Databricks projects.Code Modularity & Reusability1. Python Scripts Enable Better ModularizationPython scripts are better suited for structured, modular codebases due to their -✔ Support for project folder structures and reusable modules.✔ Ability to import external and custom modules efficiently.✔ Seamless integration with software engineering best practices (unit tests, dependency injection, and OOP principles).Example: Modularizing Python scripts in Databricks📂 databricks_project ├── 📂 modules │ ├── data_loader.py ├── main.pyTo import and reuse modules -from modules.data_loader import load_datadf = load_data("/mnt/data/raw.csv")Using modular imports makes Python scripts more maintainable and scalable.2. Notebooks Encourage Exploratory, Monolithic Code StructuresNotebooks promote exploratory programming, but they often lead to monolithic, less reusable code due to -Cell-based execution, where functions and variables are defined inline rather than structured as reusable modules.Lack of clear project organization, making it harder to refactor or extend code.Difficulties in reusing code across multiple projects without duplication.Example: A monolithic notebook structure -# Data Loading (Cell 1)df = spark.read.csv("/mnt/data/raw.csv")# Data Processing (Cell 2)df_cleaned = df.dropna()# Model Training (Cell 3)from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifiermodel = RandomForestClassifier().fit(df_cleaned)This approach lacks modularization, making it difficult to reuse components across different projects.3. Using Import In Databricks — Workspace Modules Vs. FileStoreTo reuse code across notebooks in Databricks, users can -Store Python modules in Databricks Workspace (Workspace/Shared/) and import them using %run.Use Databricks FileStore (dbfs:/) for managing .py modules as files.Example: Importing a workspace module inside a notebook -%run "/Workspace/Shared/utilities"Example: Importing a FileStore module -import syssys.path.append("/dbfs/Shared/")However, this approach does not scale well for larger projects, making Python scripts a better choice for structured development.1. Debugging In Notebooks — UI-Based Logging & VisualizationNotebooks provide built-in debugging features, such as -✔ Inline visualization for immediate feedback.✔ Execution history tracking in the UI.✔ Logging via display() and Databricks widgets.Example: Debugging with inline visualization -display(df) # Visualizing a Spark DataFrame in the UIAre UI-bound, making them harder to persist in external logging frameworks.Do not integrate natively with breakpoints, making step-by-step debugging harder.2. Python Scripts — Easier Integration With Logging FrameworksPython scripts support structured logging using logging or MLflow for experiment tracking, enabling -✔ Persisting logs in external systems (e.g., Datadog, ELK, Azure Monitor).✔ Granular logging levels (DEBUG, INFO, WARNING, ERROR).✔ More robust debugging using IDE-based breakpoints.Example: Logging with Python’s logging module -import logginglogging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)logger.info("Processing started")This allows logs to be persisted outside of Databricks, improving traceability and observability.3. Debugging Python Scripts Via IDEs (PyCharm, VSCode)Unlike notebooks, Python scripts can be debugged using breakpoints in IDEs -Set breakpoints to pause execution and inspect variables.Use interactive debugging with step-through execution.Profile CPU and memory usage for optimization.Example: Running A Databricks script In debug mode -python -m debugpy --listen 5678 --wait-for-client my_script.pyThis approach enables more precise debugging compared to notebooks.Version control is one of the biggest challenges when managing Databricks notebooks in collaborative development environments.1.Challenges In Tracking Notebook Diffs (.dbc Vs. .py)Example: Tracking changes in notebooks via Git can result in large, unreadable diffs -- "execution_count": 12,+ "execution_count": 13,- "outputs": [{"output_type": "stream", "text": ["Old output"]}],+ "outputs": [{"output_type": "stream", "text": ["New output"]}]This makes code reviews difficult, compared to the clean diffs in Python scripts.2. Using Databricks Repos For Git IntegrationDatabricks introduced Repos to provide native Git integration, allowing -✔ Syncing notebooks with Git repositories (GitHub, Azure DevOps, Bitbucket).✔ Using feature branching for collaborative development.✔ Tracking changes via version control.Example: Linking a Databricks Repo to GitHub -databricks repos create --url https://github.com/org/repo.git --path /Repos/my-repoThis improves CI/CD workflows, but Python scripts remain a better fit for software engineering best practices.3. Python Scripts — Natural Fit For CI/CD PipelinesPython scripts integrate seamlessly with CI/CD tools, enabling -✔ Unit testing with pytest and automated quality checks.✔ Git-based workflows for structured code reviews.✔ Automated deployment via Jenkins, GitHub Actions, or Azure DevOps.Example: Automating unit tests in Databricks Python scripts -pytest tests/ --junitxml=results.xmlBy adopting Python scripts for production workloads, teams gain better CI/CD integration and development rigor.When considering development and maintainability, the key trade-offs are -✔ Python scripts provide better modularization, debugging, and CI/CD integration.✔ Notebooks excel in interactive workflows but introduce maintainability challenges.✔ Version control and structured logging are significantly easier with Python scripts.For long-term maintainability and production deployments, Python scripts offer greater flexibility and scalability, while notebooks remain valuable for prototyping and exploratory analysis.Best Use Cases For Each ApproachThe decision between Databricks notebooks and Python scripts depends on the specific workload requirements, collaboration needs, and performance constraints. While notebooks offer an intuitive, interactive experience, Python scripts provide scalability, modularity, and better integration with production workflows.This section outlines the ideal use cases for each approach, helping teams determine when to use notebooks for exploration and Python scripts for automation and large-scale processing.Databricks notebooks are designed for interactive development, visualization, and incremental execution.
