They are best suited for scenarios that require exploratory workflows, real-time debugging, and collaboration.1. Interactive Data Exploration, Visualization, & ExperimentationNotebooks excel in exploratory data analysis (EDA) and visualization, enabling -✔ Real-time execution and stateful data exploration.✔ Interactive visualizations using display() and built-in charting.✔ Integration with libraries like matplotlib, seaborn, and plotly.Example: Visualizing data in a Databricks notebook -import matplotlib.pyplot as pltdf = spark.read.csv("/mnt/data/sample.csv", header=True, inferSchema=True)df_pd = df.toPandas()plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))plt.hist(df_pd["column_name"], bins=30)plt.title("Distribution of Column")plt.show()Since notebooks retain execution state, they allow users to incrementally refine analyses without reloading the entire dataset.2. Ad-Hoc Queries & Collaborative Development In Databricks UIDatabricks notebooks provide a centralized workspace for teams to -Run SQL, Python, and Scala queries interactively.Collaborate on data processing workflows.Share results using Markdown (%md) and visual widgets.Example: Running SQL queries directly inside a Python notebook -%sqlSELECT * FROM sales_data WHERE revenue > 10000;This enables quick iteration over data queries without requiring standalone job execution.3. ML Model Prototyping & Incremental DebuggingNotebooks are widely used for machine learning (ML) model development due to -✔ Step-by-step debugging without restarting the execution environment.✔ Experiment tracking with tools like MLflow.✔ Integration with AutoML and hyperparameter tuning frameworks.Example: Incremental model training in a notebook -from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifiermodel = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100)model.fit(X_train, y_train)print(model.score(X_test, y_test))Unlike Python scripts, notebooks retain state, allowing for iterative refinements without reloading datasets.When To Use Python ScriptsPython scripts are better suited for production workloads, ensuring scalability, efficiency, and maintainability.1. Production Pipelines & Batch Job AutomationFor scheduled jobs and repeatable ETL processes, Python scripts provide -✔ Consistent execution without manual intervention.✔ Integration with Databricks Workflows and REST APIs.✔ Automated cluster management with ephemeral execution.Example: Defining a batch ETL job in Databricks -{ "name": "ETLJob", "spark_version": "12.0.x-scala2.12", "node_type_id": "Standard_DS3_v2", "num_workers": 4 "spark_python_task": { "python_file": "dbfs:/scripts/etl_process.py" }By executing as a batch job, Python scripts reduce memory overhead compared to notebooks.2. Large-Scale Data Processing Workloads Requiring Performance OptimizationPython scripts are more efficient for processing massive datasets, as they -✔ Avoid UI-layer overheads present in notebooks.✔ Leverage multiprocessing and Spark parallelism efficiently.✔ Enable fine-grained resource management (auto-scaling, ephemeral execution).Example: Parallelizing data processing with Python scripts -from multiprocessing import Pooldef process_partition(data_partition): # Process data return data_partition.count()# Split data into partitionsdata_partitions = df.randomSplit([0.5, 0.5]) results = p.map(process_partition, data_partitions)This allows Python scripts to fully utilize Databricks clusters for distributed execution.3. CI/CD & Software Development Best PracticesFor enterprise-grade software development, Python scripts enable -✔ Modular, reusable codebases (structured project directories, imports).✔ Version control integration with GitHub, Bitbucket, or Azure DevOps.✔ Automated testing with pytest and CI/CD pipelines.Example: Running automated unit tests for Python scripts -pytest tests/ --junitxml=results.xmlPython scripts align more naturally with DevOps practices, ensuring scalability and maintainability.The best execution mode depends on the workload requirements -For interactive workflows and rapid experimentation, notebooks are ideal.For production workloads, CI/CD pipelines, and large-scale processing, Python scripts are the better choice.Benchmarking Python Scripts Vs. Notebooks In DatabricksTo objectively compare Databricks notebooks and Python scripts, we conducted a performance benchmark focused on execution speed, memory consumption, and cluster resource utilization. The results highlight the strengths and weaknesses of each approach for ETL jobs, batch processing, and large-scale data workloads.To compare Databricks notebooks and Python scripts in terms of -✔ — How long each execution mode takes to complete an ETL job.✔ — How much cluster memory is consumed during execution.✔ — The impact on CPU and Spark executor utilization.2. Workload Definition — ETL Pipeline ExecutionThe experiment involved -Reading a 100GB dataset from Delta Lake.Performing transformations using Spark (filtering, aggregations, joins).Writing processed data back to Delta Lake.The same logic was executed in both a Databricks notebook and a standalone Python script.1.Execution Speed ComparisonNotebooks have higher startup latency due to UI and execution context initialization.Python scripts execute faster as they bypass UI-related overheads and run in an optimized batch mode.2. Memory Usage & Resource AllocationNotebooks retain execution contexts between cells, leading to higher memory consumption.Python scripts release resources immediately after execution, reducing long-term memory overhead.Python batch jobs utilize cluster executors more efficiently, achieving higher parallelism.3. Scalability For Large DatasetsPython scripts scale better with larger datasets, avoiding execution context memory leaks seen in notebooks.Notebooks struggle with memory-intensive workloads due to persistent variable storage.Batch execution mode in Python scripts allows more efficient resource scaling.The same ETL job was executed in both a Databricks notebook and a standalone Python script.1.Running An ETL Job In A Databricks Notebook# Read 100GB datasetdf = spark.read.format("delta").load("/mnt/delta/large_dataset")# Perform transformationsdf_transformed = df.filter("column_x > 100").groupBy("column_y").agg({"column_z": "sum"})# Write back to Delta Lakedf_transformed.write.format("delta").mode("overwrite").save("/mnt/delta/processed_data")Notebook Execution Observations:✔ UI provides inline execution monitoring.✔ Cell-by-cell execution allows stepwise debugging.✘ Memory usage increases due to persistent execution context.✘ Execution time is slightly longer due to UI overhead.2. Running The Same ETL Job As A Python Scriptfrom pyspark.sql import SparkSessionspark = SparkSession.builder.appName("ETLJob").getOrCreate()df = spark.read.format("delta").load("/mnt/delta/large_dataset")df_transformed = df.filter("column_x > 100").groupBy("column_y").agg({"column_z": "sum"})df_transformed.write.format("delta").mode("overwrite").save("/mnt/delta/processed_data")Executed as a batch job -Python Script Execution Observations:✔ Faster execution by bypassing UI overhead.✔ Lower memory footprint due to ephemeral execution.✔ Can be integrated into CI/CD pipelines for scheduled automation.✘ Requires external logging setup for debugging.✔ Databricks notebooks are best for interactive development, data exploration, and incremental debugging.✔ Python scripts provide better performance, lower memory overhead, and higher scalability for production workloads.✔ For large-scale processing and batch automation, Python scripts outperform notebooks due to optimized execution modes.Choosing between Databricks notebooks and Python scripts is a critical decision that impacts execution efficiency, scalability, and maintainability. While notebooks provide an intuitive, interactive development environment, Python scripts excel in performance, modularity, and CI/CD integration.This article has explored performance benchmarks, execution trade-offs, and best practices to help data engineers and architects optimize their Databricks workloads.By leveraging these best practices and optimization techniques, our Databricks specialists at Xinthe have helped our clientele -✔ Improve Databricks execution efficiency and reduce cluster costs.✔ Structure projects effectively for modular, maintainable Python workloads.✔ Optimize Spark performance to handle large-scale datasets efficiently.

item.title: How I Contributed to Kestra: A Beginner’s Perspective
item.author: Pravesh Sudha
item.content:encoded: Welcome to the world of Open Source! Today, I’m excited to share my experience of contributing to an amazing open-source project — Kestra.For those unfamiliar, Kestra is an open-source platform designed for orchestrating data and automating workflows, much like data pipelines. What makes it special? You can define workflows in YAML format, making it highly developer-friendly, while its no-code interface allows non-developers to automate tasks effortlessly.One of the things I love most about Kestra is its open-source nature and its philosophy — “Start simple and grow as needed.” Getting started with Kestra is incredibly easy. You can spin it up on your local system using Docker with just one command:docker run --pull=always --rm -it -p 8080:8080 --user=root \-v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock -v /tmp:/tmp \kestra/kestra:latest server localWith that, you’re all set to explore the power of workflow automation!💡 My First Issue: Fixing Pagination ColorsContributing to open source for the first time can feel overwhelming, but trust me — it’s an exciting journey! My first issue in Kestra was a UI-related task that involved fixing pagination colors in the Vue files. You can check out my contribution here.The issue? The colors of the page numbers in the pagination component were not aligned with the design system. It might seem like a small change, but good UI/UX plays a crucial role in making an application feel polished.To resolve this issue, I made the following updates in the Pagination.vue file:Adjusted the color of total pages using Vue’s CSS variables:--ks-content-secondary/primary2. Added logic for hover and active page color, ensuring that the UI looked more interactive and visually consistent.This task might seem minor, but it was a great first step to understanding how Kestra’s UI components work. It also gave me confidence in navigating an open-source codebase and making meaningful changes.💡 My Second Issue: Adding kestraFilter to Flow TriggersAfter successfully fixing the pagination colors, I moved on to my next issue — adding the kestraFilter to the Trigger section of Flows. At first, I misunderstood the task and ended up modifying the position of the Refresh button instead. Thankfully, Milos Paunovic, a Senior Frontend Engineer at , patiently guided me toward the actual requirement.Understanding the ProblemAfter carefully analyzing the UI, I realized that the Kestra filter was already implemented in multiple components across the application. The goal was to bring this filter functionality to the Flow Trigger section, allowing users to search and filter triggers efficiently.Created the KestraFilter in the FlowTrigger.vue file.Added a searchTrigger function to handle filtering logic.However, things didn’t go smoothly at first. For some reason, the search functionality wasn’t working as expected. I debugged it for a while but couldn’t pinpoint the exact issue. That’s when Milos stepped in to help, made a few minor tweaks, and — boom! — the filter was up and running.This issue taught me an important lesson about debugging — sometimes, even a small change can break functionality, and it’s okay to ask for help. Open-source contributions are not just about writing code; they’re also about collaborating, learning from experienced developers, and improving problem-solving skills.My open-source journey with has been nothing short of amazing! A huge shoutout to Kunal Kushwaha — I first learned about Kestra from one of his video, and that led me to explore and contribute to this fantastic project.The Kestra team has been incredibly responsive and welcoming, making my experience smooth and enjoyable. Special thanks to Milos Paunovic, whose minor tweaks taught me valuable lessons about debugging and refining code. This experience has motivated me to contribute even more and continue learning with the team.For anyone interested in workflow automation, I highly recommend checking out Kestra! If you ever get stuck, don’t hesitate to join the Kestra Slack channel and ask your questions in the appropriate channel — you’ll find a supportive community ready to help.Happy coding, and see you in the open-source world! 🚀

item.title: Optimize JVM for kubernetes (part 1)
item.author: Vivek Malhotra
item.content:encoded: Today we would discuss how JVM actually behaves when running in a container environment like kubertenes. I would also share how we setup our jvm to run on kubernetes in our organization. I have also created a github repo to follow up what I am doing and try small examples yourself.The topics I intend to cover in this two part series will be:How JVM thread pools, garbage collector and heap memory max size (referred as heap size from here) are initialized based on k8s resource requests and limits on the containerWhat jvm options you can provide to tune these params when running your application in a containerLastly, how cpu throttling works (covered in upcoming Part 2)You can access all the artifacts in this article on the github repo here.JVM memory management in a containerWe all know as a Java developer that understanding how works is so important to tune performance of your application. A production application if not tuned correctly will not scale under load and can end up having latency issues. If you are working with modern reactive frameworks like RxJava or even Virtual threads they are eventually executed on a OS thread.Most of these frameworks are implemented via forkJoinPool which internally will call this piece of code to determine the size of thread pool.Runtime.getRuntime().availableProcessors()What value this method returns is based on how JVM perceives the number of processors available to it. This is a native method so would ideally look at the OS cores. If you are running on a container in k8s this will return the cpu resources allocated to your container. If you are kotlin developer it still uses this piece of code internally to size the dispatcher pools to execute coroutines.The other part of the memory management is . Garbage collectors come in different flavours and had an evolution of their own. If left on their own JVM chooses garbage collector based on the heap size and cpu resources. G1GC and ZGC are the latest garbage collectors you can choose from.Lastly the itself. You will create millions of objects in your Java application. Your applications heap will size traditionally on the (initial) and (limit) sizes. However it is recommended to use flag which assigns heap size based on % of the memory of the container.Now lets run some kubernetes examples and see how resource requests and limits impact size of thread pool, choice of garbage collector and heap size.Test setup for running jvm on k8sI wrote a small class which prints lots of useful information like available processors that JVM sees, the choosen GC and heap size. We will basically run this code in a container image and also have a small http server listening on 8081 port. This was done so that pod is not killed by the kubelet and JVM is not exited. I created a to package the jvm-stats/target/jvm-stats.jar with container image.FROM docker.io/library/openjdk:21-jdkCOPY jvm-stats/target/jvm-stats.jar /app/jvm-stats.jarI setup my local single node k8s cluster using podman and minikube. You are free to setup how you please. I have also provide my minikube config under .minikube/config/config.json in the repo.{ "container-runtime": "crio", "driver": "podman",}run on your local machine. build the Dockerfile and upload to minikube local docker registry with:#!/bin/shminikube image build -f ./Dockerfile -t jvm-stats:1.0 .Now we are going to deploy variations of pod configurations with different resource request and limits and assign arguments to command which is entrypoint to the container. Then we will poll logs of each pod and see jvm statistics as seen by the jvm.— When request and limit values of cpu and memory are sameapiVersion: v1kind: Pod name: jvm-stats1spec: - image: docker.io/library/jvm-stats:1.0 imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent name: jvm-stats1 requests: memory: 1Gi cpu: 1000m args: - "/app/jvm-stats.jar"The request values basically mean your pod/container will be allocated to a k8s node which has 1000m of cpu and 1Gi of memory available. Also the limit values mean the container will be caped to this memory and cpu. Deploy this pod and later check its logs:kubectl apply -f jvm-stats1.yamlkubectl logs pod/jvm-stats1The logs of the pod/container will begin with these lines of code (ignore the rest of the logs)Total Available Processors: 1Garbage Collectors in use:- MarkSweepCompactTotal Heap Memory (Max): 247 MBLet us try to understand what is happening here. Available processors count that JVM sees is 1 which is cpu request/limit. Heap size is 247 MB which is 25% of memory request/limit. Garbage collector selected is MarkSweepCompact.Available processor count is decided based on the cpu resources of container. Heap size by default is initialized to approx 25% of the container memory unless specified with jvm options. This means it is not sufficient enough to run your jvm application as as this will definitely initialize your jvm insufficiently. It is also not smart choice to hard-code the - and values in Dockerfile since you would like to run your application with a variation of memory resources in different environments like test or production.jvm-stats2.yaml — Now lets see the jvm statistics if we remove cpu and memory limits.containers:- image: docker.io/library/jvm-stats:1.0 imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent name: jvm-stats2 requests: memory: 1Gi - "/app/JvmStatisticsInfo.java"Here the output will be based on the resources your chosen node has.Total Available Processors: 5Garbage Collectors in use:- G1 Concurrent GCTotal Heap Memory (Max): 1840 MBWoow, the available processors count is 5 and it is not equal to the value. Since there are no limits.cpu specified here the jvm sees the available number of cores provided by kernel. Also the heap size was initialized to 25% total memory available to the node.If no cpu and memory limits are provided to the container the thread pools and heap size will be initialized according to the cpu and memory of the node.Lets see next how to really set the memory resources on a container.This is how you should initialize the heap size in jvm and provide memory requests and limit valuesresources: requests: memory: 2Gi cpu: 1000margs: - "-XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75" - "-jar"deploy this pod on the k8s cluster and poll the logs.Total Available Processors: 1Garbage Collectors in use:- MarkSweepCompactTotal Heap Memory (Max): 1484 MBNotice, that we now have same memory request and limits of 2Gi and we are running our app with option. This initializes your heap with a determined heap memory size. This is one of the prerequisites of classifying your pod with Also notice that Garbage collector is still assgined to use old MarkSweep GC. values are used by kubelet to choose a node to provision your pod on it. Kubelet takes a sum of cpu/memory values of all containers. values are an indicator that node will reserve this much memory for your container. values are used as weightage when assigning computation slice at a time when competing process threads are waiting to be scheduled. cpu requests are also used by Horizontal Pod Autoscaler to calculate the desired replicas of your pod.A node can commit more than 100% of its resources to cpu and memory limits. memory limit is used by jvm to calculate the heap size. The you see in the logs is max limit of heap size. This does not necessary mean your app is using this memory. If the jvm tries to allocate more memory and your node is running low on memory and is starved, kubelet will start killing containers based on the Quality of Service class assigned to the pods on this node. You can check the QoS class with kubectl describe pod/jvm-stats2. Details of QoS classes are documented hereAssign the same value of memory and cpu requests limits to your container classifies it as . This way kubelet will consider your container to be killed preemptly only when no other lower class of containers are running on the node. At the very least assign the same memory requests and limits to your container. This will make sure the node will reserve as much memory for your jvm heap and kernel will not try to reclaim part of it. Also use or even 85 to initialize heap size to use 75% of memory limits.cpu limits are a different story. The defines a hard ceiling on how much CPU time the container can use. During each scheduling interval (time slice), the Linux kernel checks to see if this limit is exceeded; if so, the kernel waits before allowing that cgroup to resume execution.jvm-stats4.yaml — have a more deterministic Garbage collector.We wrote earlier that if you leave the choice of collector to the jvm then garbage collector you get is based on available cores and heap size. Most production applications today will find it useful to use either G1 garbage collector or ZGC garbage collector. All available garbage collectors in jdk 23 are documented here.resources: requests: memory: 2Gi cpu: 1000margs: - "-XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75" - "-XX:+UseZGC" - "-jar"The logs of the container would beTotal Available Processors: 1Garbage Collectors in use:- ZGC Minor Pauses- ZGC Major PausesTotal Heap Memory (Max): 1536 MBTo have a more determined GC for your jvm use either or -XX:+UseZGC -XX:+ZGenerationalNow lets see how to influence the threap pool sizes in jvmThis file has these cpu requests and limitsresources: requests: limits:Deploy it to k8s and the output will be Total Available Processors: 2 ││ Garbage Collectors in use: ││ - ZGC Minor Cycles ││ - ZGC Minor Pauses ││ - ZGC Major Cycles ││ - ZGC Major Pauses ││ Total Heap Memory (Max): 1536 MBThe threal pool will be sized to the cpu limits not the requests. This does not mean your jvm has complete and exclusive access to 2 cpu cores of node. The cpu usage is throttled based on the cpu limits. If you set memory requests and limits to same value and have different cpu request and limits your pod will be assigned . This class is lower than the Guaranteed and will be preempted next if no pod of is present on the node.jvm-stats6.yaml — What if you want to increase the concurrency with limited cpu resources.What if you assign a cpu limit of 1 to container but still want the default thread pools in jvm to be initialized to more threads? try this yaml which has this configresources: requests: memory: 2Gi cpu: 1000margs: - "-XX:ActiveProcessorCount=4" - "-XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75" - "-XX:+ZGenerational" - "/app/jvm-stats.jar"Total Available Processors: 4Garbage Collectors in use:- ZGC Minor Pauses- ZGC Major PausesTotal Heap Memory (Max): 1536 MBuse to overrides the number of CPUs that the jvm will use to calculate the size of thread pools it will use for various operations such as Garbage Collection and ForkJoinPool.This article is part 1 of the series to understand how jvm memory management and thread pools can be optimized to production k8s cluster. I will publish part 2 of this series to explain how cpu throttling works. Do try these examples out and drop some feedback if you think I missed any information out.

item.title: 25 Essential Docker Commands for Beginners to Advanced Users
item.author: Harikrishnan M
item.content:encoded: Docker has revolutionized software deployment by enabling developers to package applications into containers. Whether you’re a beginner or an advanced user, mastering Docker commands is essential. In this blog, we’ll cover 25 essential Docker commands, from basic to advanced, with examples.Let’s start with some fundamental commands to set up and manage containers.👉 Displays the installed Docker version.2️⃣ List Docker System Information👉 Provides system-wide Docker information.👉 Authenticates Docker Hub credentials.👉 Searches for an image on Docker Hub.👉 Downloads the latest Nginx image.6️⃣ List Downloaded Images👉 Displays locally stored images.docker run -d -p 8080:80 nginx👉 Runs an Nginx container in detached mode, exposing port 80 on 8080.8️⃣ List Running Containers👉 Shows currently running containers.9️⃣ List All Containers (Running & Stopped)👉 Displays all containers.🔟 Stop a Running Container👉 Stops a specific container.🔹 Intermediate Docker CommandsMoving to more useful commands for managing containers and images.1️⃣1️⃣ Remove a Stopped Container👉 Deletes a specific container.1️⃣3️⃣ Remove All Stopped Containers👉 Cleans up all stopped containers.1️⃣4️⃣ Remove Unused Images👉 Deletes unused Docker images.1️⃣5️⃣ View Container Logs👉 Shows logs for a specific container.1️⃣6️⃣ Execute Commands in a Running Containerdocker exec -it container_id bash👉 Accesses the container shell interactively.1️⃣7️⃣ Copy Files to/from a Containerdocker cp myfile.txt container_id:/myfolder/👉 Copies files into a container.docker cp container_id:/myfolder/myfile.txt .👉 Copies files from a container.1️⃣8️⃣ Restart a Containerdocker restart container_id👉 Restarts a specific container.1️⃣9️⃣ View Running Process in a Container👉 Shows active processes inside a container.2️⃣0️⃣ Inspect Container Detailsdocker inspect container_id👉 Provides detailed information about a container.🔹 Advanced Docker CommandsNow, let’s look at commands that help in orchestration, networking, and persistent storage.2️⃣1️⃣ Run a Container with Volume (Persistent Data)docker run -d -p 3306:3306 --name mysql-db -v mysql-data:/var/lib/mysql -e MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD=root mysql👉 Runs a MySQL container with persistent data storage.2️⃣2️⃣ Build a Custom Image from Dockerfiledocker build -t my-custom-app .👉 Builds a Docker image from a Dockerfile.docker tag my-custom-app myrepo/my-custom-app:v1👉 Tags an image for pushing to Docker Hub.2️⃣4️⃣ Push an Image to Docker Hubdocker push myrepo/my-custom-app:v1👉 Uploads a tagged image to Docker Hub.2️⃣5️⃣ Create and Use a Docker Networkdocker network create my_networkdocker run -d --name app --network my_network nginxdocker run -d --name db --network my_network mysql👉 Creates a custom Docker network and runs containers within it.Mastering these 25 essential Docker commands will help you efficiently work with containers, manage images, and deploy applications. Whether you are just starting or diving deeper into DevOps, these commands are crucial.🚀 Have a favourite Docker command? Share it in the comments! 🐳

item.title: Splunk ITSI & Prometheus — a match made in heaven?
item.author: ObsNSods
item.content:encoded: Splunk ITSI & Prometheus — a match made in heaven?Prometheus is a hugely popular open source metric collection and alerting platform many companies are using to provide observability and monitoring of their platforms and services.Although prometheus is a very strong metric collection and alerting tool it doesn’t offer great options to visualise metric and alert data or to correlate metrics and alerts with other sources.There are already documented solutions to bring the metrics Prometheus collects into Splunk but this particular article aims to document a simple process to bring only alerts from Prometheus Alertmanager into ITSI episodes.Managing all your disparate alerting sources in ITSI offers great benefits for operations staff and also allows the user to correlate more traditional alerting with service-centric views.In our example we have Prometheus collecting metrics from our Linux estate. These metrics (CPU, Memory etc.) are driving alerting for the infrastructure.We would like to bring any open alerts into ITSI as episodes so we can manage these alerts from the same place as we monitor our services. In addition, we would like to know if one of our services has an open episode.An overview of Prometheus and its functionality, as well as low level detail on installation and configuration is beyond the scope of this document but if you wish to learn more about some of the configuration mentioned in the steps below please read the official Prometheus documentation which can be found here: https://prometheus.io/docs/introduction/overview/The intention of this paper is to outline a general approach and focus on getting the data in and configuring ITSI to enable the integration.That said, in this section we will document a very simple prometheus configuration to enable a user to understand the high-level steps involved.Prometheus generally uses a pull-based mechanism for collecting metrics. It “scrapes” targets at set intervals to collect the metrics they provide and stores them. Targets in this sense are generally (but not always) small programs called exporters running on hosts that publish their metrics on an http endpoint. It is those http endpoints that are called scrapetargets.Prometheus also has a bolt-on tool called Alertmanager. Rules are created on Prometheus to evaluate metrics (i.e Disk Space > 80%) and if there is a breach they are sent on to Alertmanager for processing.Below is the official Prometheus diagram. For the purposes of this example we are talking about the “Prometheus server” box and the “Prometheus targets” along with the “Alertmanager”.The example we will use is alerting on whether a scrape target is down. This means Prometheus cannot connect to the instance to collect metrics. This might mean the exporter is down or, more seriously the whole host is down.The installation of exporters is beyond the scope of this article but detailed in the next sections is the prometheus configuration required in order to generate an alert in alertmanager stating a scrape target is no longer responding.Defining the Scrape TargetThe following configuration is added to the prometheus configuration file:scrape_configs:# The job name is added as a label `job=<job_name>` to any timeseries scraped fromthis config.- job_name: 'node_exporter'static_configs:- targets: ['adalton-ds2:9100']This tells Prometheus to scrape the host adalton-ds2 on port 9100. The job name is called node_exporter. The node_exporter has already been installed and is serving up metrics on the http port 9100.Now we need to define an alert rule to determine whether the scrape target is available or not.groups:- name: AllInstances- alert: InstanceDownfor: 1mtitle: 'Instance {{ $labels.instance }} down'description: '{{ $labels.instance }} of job {{ $labels.job }} has been down for morethan one minute'severity: 'critical'The alert is defined in Prometheus’ own query language PromQL.For this example, we will assume Alertmanager and the configuration for prometheus to send to Alertmanager has been carried out. Details on this step can be found here:So, we now have an alert that will be sent to Alertmanager, but how do we get those alerts into ITSI?We will be configuring Alertmanager to send alert status events to Splunk via the HTTP Event Collector.Splunk will require an HTTP Event Collector token created and ideally a new index. For the purposes of this example assume we have created a new index called “prometheus” and a new HTTP Event Collector token also called “prometheus”, setting a sourcetype of prometheus, that has supplied the following token:277dc2d6–29f9–4f26–9ccf-2c5032d7f632Alertmanager ConfigurationNow we need to configure Alertmanager to send status events to Splunk. We do this by updating its main configuration file with a block similar to below:route:group_by: ['alertname']group_interval: 10sreceiver: 'splunk'- name: 'splunk'- url:'http://your_splunk_server:8088/services/collector/raw?channel=00872DC6-AC83-4EDE-8AFE-8413C3825C4C'http_config:insecure_skip_verify: trueusername: Splunkpassword: 277dc2d6-29f9-4f26-9ccf-2c5032d7f632There is no official integration from Prometheus to Splunk so we have used Prometheus’ generic webhook config.We have specified a repeat_interval of 1m — This tells Alertmanager to send status alerts each minute if there is an open alert. Status changes (Up to Down, Down to Up etc.) will be sent immediately. We want a constant flow of status when an alert has fired to enable us to craft our ITSI search later.You’ll notice the url config item is a url specifying the HTTP Event Collector. Note the use of the raw endpoint. This is required for the Prometheus events. If you use the raw endpoint you must specify a channel.The basic_auth section is the usual authentication headers passed to the HTTP Event Collector. Password being the HTTP Event Collector token created in the step “Splunk Configuration”.Full details on the specific configuration options for Alertmanager can be found here:We now need to configure ITSI to handle the alert data, create distinct episodes from that data and apply any rules necessary to manage the episode.In this simple example we are going to have ITSI close the episode should no new alert data be sent. If you recall, we ask Alertmanager to send repeat alert events when there is an open alert each minute. If we do not receive this alert data we will assume the alert has cleared and close the ITSI episode accordingly.In order to create an episode ITSI requires a correlation search to be created. The output of a correlation search creates a Notable Event. Notable events can then be grouped into episodes via Notable Event Aggregation Policies (NEAP).Let’s first take a look at the event data generated by Alertmanager so we can then craft our search.Alertmanager will send a JSON payload for each open alert that looks something like this:{"receiver":"splunk","status":"firing","alerts":[{"status":"firing","labels":{"alertname":"InstanceDown","instance":"adalton-ds2:9100","job":"node_exporter","severity":"critical"},"annotations":{"description":"adalton-ds2:9100 of job node_exporter has been down for more than oneminute","title":"Instance adalton-ds2:9100down"},"startsAt":"2021-04-09T13:57:23.952Z","endsAt":"0001-01-01T00:00:00Z","generatorURL":"http://adalton-ds2:9090/graph?g0.expr=up+%3D%3D+0\u0026g0.tab=1","fingerprint":"3fd82a5efb6f3b1e"}],"groupLabels":{"alertname":"InstanceDown"},"commonLabels":{"alertname":"InstanceDown","instance":"adalton-ds2:9100","job":"node_exporter","severity":"critical"},"commonAnnotations":{"description":"adalton-ds2:9100 of jobnode_exporter has been down for more than one minute","title":"Instancedown"},"externalURL":"http://adalton-ds2:9093","version":"4","groupKey":"{}:{alertname=\"InstanceDown\"}","truncatedAlerts":0}To create the correlation search navigate to ITSI -> Configuration -> Correlation Searches…We have called the search “Prometheus — Instance Down”We have selected “Ad hoc” and supplied the following search:index=prometheus groupKey=*InstanceDown* alerts{}.status=firing | sort 0 -_time | stats list(*) by _time | mvexpand list(alerts{}.annotations.description) |rename list(alerts{}.annotations.description) as alert_description | rexfield=alert_description "^(?<instance>.+\:.+)\s.+job\s(?<job_name>.+)\sha.+" |eval host_entity=mvindex(split(instance,":"),0) | fields alert_description,instance, host_entity, job_name, list(alerts{}.labels.severity) | renamelist(alerts{}.labels.severity) as alert_severity | eval severity=6, status=0,description="A Prometheus scrape instance is down: " . instance . " job name:" . job_name, instruction="Check alertmanager athttp://10.202.33.172:9093/#/alerts?silenced=false&inhibited=false&active=true&filter=%7Balertname%3D%22InstanceDown%22%7D",event_source="prometheus" | fields - alert_severityThe search will look back over the last 5 minutes.We associate the episode with the “Linux” service (this service has previously been created)We associate the field host_entity field with an entity in ITSI — This field is an evaluated field in the search above to manipulate a prometheus label in order to match an ITSI entityWe specify the ITSI severity field — This is another evaluated field in the search aboveWe have also created a field that links the user back to the Prometheus alert manager for extra information. This URL will be displayed in ITSI to aid users to troubleshoot an episodePerhaps the most important field is “Notable Event Identifier Fields” — This tells ITSI the field to use to discern whether a notable event is unique or not. In this example we are using the alert_description field as we want to group alerts of the same type from the same host. All this detail is in the alert_description field so we can be happy that unique notable events will be created for each hostIn summary, each 5 minutes we will search the prometheus index for:Any alert that has a groupKey containing “InstanceDown” with a status of “firing”. If you recall this is the name of the alert we created in the section above “Defining the alert”We set a field (host_entity) containing an instance name that will match an already discovered ITSI entity (in this case adalton-ds2)We create a meaningful, unique alert_description field that ITSI will use to discern unique eventsWe manually set an ITSI severity level field (severity) and set it to ITSI severity 6 (Critical)The Noteable Event Aggregation Policy (NEAP)Now we have created notable events we need to tell ITSI how to handle those events and apply sensible correlation in order to aid operations staff who are managing the events.The rules applied by the NEAP create ITSI episodes.To create a NEAP, navigate to ITSI -> Configuration -> Notable Event Aggregation PoliciesWe have set the following filtering criteria for our NEAP:Include events from the source “prometheus”Split those events into episodes dependent on the alert_description field. In our example that means we will get separate episodes should the alert fire on different hostsWe choose to break the episode if there has been a pause of 6 minutes. Remember, Alertmanager will send status for open alerts every minute, so if we haven’t received status in 6 minutes we can assume that episode is over. Be aware, breaking an episode is not the same as closing an episode. Breaking an episode just means that if notable events started coming in 6 minutes after the last matching notable event, a new episode would be createdNext, we set “Action rules” — this tells ITSI how to handle the episode, including any automation you may want to run:In our simple example we will:Change the status of the episode to resolved if we don’t receive any notable events for 400 secondsChange the status of the episode to closed if we don’t receive any notable events for 600 secondsWe have manually stopped the node_exporter process on our scrape target. We should now see an alert fire on Prometheus and the alert be forwarded to Alertmanager which will in turn feed Splunk open alert data.This is a screenshot of the alert firing on prometheus:This is a screenshot of the alert received at Alertmanager:This is a screenshot of the alert event being received at Splunk:This screenshot shows the correlation search output:The corresponding episode on ITSI:You’ll notice the corresponding service has also been highlighted in the drilldown.You’ll also notice that the episode is not contributing negatively to the service health but is indicated with an exclamation mark in the service view in service analyzer:It is possible to have episodes contribute to service health score but that configuration is out of scope for this article.By selecting the “Add Filter” option in Episode Review, and then choosing “Policy” followed by our NEAP policy we created (in this case “Prometheus”) we can see the episodes that are being controlled by that policy:As you can see the policy we created has the correct filtering applied to include our alert. This means that once we restart the node_exporter component the policy should resolve the episode after 400 seconds, and then close the episode after 600 seconds.Clicking on the episode and selecting “Activity” shows the policy updating the episode to resolved after 400 seconds:And finally, the closure after 600 seconds:Using ITSI to manage alerts from disparate monitoring and observability sources can be incredibly powerful for operations.In addition to being able to automatically group alerts any way you choose — to ensure operations teams remain focused on issues that matter — ITSI makes it incredibly easy to apply automation and correlate alerting with business services giving operations teams a truly holistic view of an important service from infrastructure right through to business KPI’s.

item.title: Kubernetes the Hard Way on Raspberry Pi, Docker & EC2 (Part 3): TLS Certificates, Encryption &…
item.author: Jaecom
item.content:encoded: Kubernetes the Hard Way on Raspberry Pi, Docker & EC2 (Part 3): TLS Certificates, Encryption & EtcdIf you missed Part 1 or Part 2, be sure to catch up before proceeding.By now, our EC2 instances and Raspberry Pi are connected via a secure WireGuard VPN, and we’ve established a jumpbox for centralized administration. In Part 3, we’ll focus on Kubernetes security and core services by:Generating and distributing TLS certificates for each Kubernetes componentCreating Kubernetes authentication configs for nodes and system componentsEncrypting sensitive data (like secrets) at restInstalling and bootstrapping etcd, the distributed key-value store backing Kubernetes1. Provisioning a CA and Generating TLS CertificatesFor Kubernetes components to communicate securely, we need to create a Certificate Authority (CA) and generate certificates for each service and node. This ensures only authorized components can talk to each other over secure channels.1.1 Create a Certificate Authority (CA)Navigate to your kubernetes-the-hard-way-raspberrypi-docker directory in your jumpbox container:cd kubernetes-the-hard-way-raspberrypi-dockerGenerate the CA key (ca.key) and certificate (ca.crt):{ openssl genrsa -out ca.key 4096 openssl req -x509 -new -sha512 -noenc \ -key ca.key -days 3653 \ -out ca.crt1.2 Generate Client and Server CertificatesWe’ll generate certificates for all Kubernetes components (nodes, controllers, proxies, etc.). This includes: (e.g., node-0, node-1)Kubernetes system components (kube-proxy, kube-scheduler, kube-controller-manager, kube-api-server, service-accounts)certs=( "admin" "node-0" "node-1" "kube-proxy" "kube-scheduler" "kube-controller-manager" "service-accounts" openssl genrsa -out "${i}.key" 4096 openssl req -new -key "${i}.key" -sha256 \ -config "ca.conf" -section ${i} \ openssl x509 -req -days 3653 -in "${i}.csr" \ -copy_extensions copyall \ -CAkey "ca.key" \ -out "${i}.crt"1.3 Distribute the CertificatesCopy the node certificates to your worker nodes (node-0 and node-1):for host in node-0 node-1; do ssh root@$host mkdir /var/lib/kubelet/ scp ca.crt root@$host:/var/lib/kubelet/ root@$host:/var/lib/kubelet/kubelet.crt root@$host:/var/lib/kubelet/kubelet.keyCopy server certificates to the control-plane node (server):scp \ ca.key ca.crt \ kube-api-server.key kube-api-server.crt \ service-accounts.key service-accounts.crt \2. Generating Kubernetes Configuration Files for AuthenticationNow that we have TLS certificates, we’ll create kubeconfig files so each component (worker nodes, kube-proxy, controllers, etc.) can securely connect to the Kubernetes API server.2.1 Generate kubeconfig file for Worker NodesGenerate kubeconfig files for each worker node (node-0 and node-1):for host in node-0 node-1; do kubectl config set-cluster kubernetes-the-hard-way \ --certificate-authority=ca.crt \ --embed-certs=true \ --server=https://server.kubernetes.local:6443 \ --kubeconfig=${host}.kubeconfig kubectl config set-credentials system:node:${host} \ --client-certificate=${host}.crt \ --client-key=${host}.key \ --embed-certs=true \ --kubeconfig=${host}.kubeconfig kubectl config set-context default \ --cluster=kubernetes-the-hard-way \ --user=system:node:${host} \ --kubeconfig=${host}.kubeconfig kubectl config use-context default \ --kubeconfig=${host}.kubeconfig2.2 Generate other config filesCreate kubeconfig files for:Each uses the CA cert and a unique client certificate/key pair:# Generate kubeconfig file for kube-proxy{ kubectl config set-cluster kubernetes-the-hard-way \ --certificate-authority=ca.crt \ --server=https://server.kubernetes.local:6443 \ --kubeconfig=kube-proxy.kubeconfig kubectl config set-credentials system:kube-proxy \ --client-certificate=kube-proxy.crt \ --client-key=kube-proxy.key \ --embed-certs=true \ --kubeconfig=kube-proxy.kubeconfig kubectl config set-context default \ --cluster=kubernetes-the-hard-way \ --user=system:kube-proxy \ --kubeconfig=kube-proxy.kubeconfig kubectl config use-context default \ --kubeconfig=kube-proxy.kubeconfig# Generate kubeconfig file for kube-controller-manager{ kubectl config set-cluster kubernetes-the-hard-way \ --certificate-authority=ca.crt \ --server=https://server.kubernetes.local:6443 \ --kubeconfig=kube-controller-manager.kubeconfig kubectl config set-credentials system:kube-controller-manager \ --client-certificate=kube-controller-manager.crt \ --client-key=kube-controller-manager.key \ --embed-certs=true \ --kubeconfig=kube-controller-manager.kubeconfig kubectl config set-context default \ --cluster=kubernetes-the-hard-way \ --user=system:kube-controller-manager \ --kubeconfig=kube-controller-manager.kubeconfig kubectl config use-context default \ --kubeconfig=kube-controller-manager.kubeconfig# Generate kubeconfig file for kube-scheduler{ kubectl config set-cluster kubernetes-the-hard-way \ --certificate-authority=ca.crt \ --server=https://server.kubernetes.local:6443 \ --kubeconfig=kube-scheduler.kubeconfig kubectl config set-credentials system:kube-scheduler \ --client-certificate=kube-scheduler.crt \ --client-key=kube-scheduler.key \ --embed-certs=true \ --kubeconfig=kube-scheduler.kubeconfig kubectl config set-context default \ --cluster=kubernetes-the-hard-way \ --user=system:kube-scheduler \ --kubeconfig=kube-scheduler.kubeconfig kubectl config use-context default \ --kubeconfig=kube-scheduler.kubeconfig# Generate kubeconfig file for admin{ kubectl config set-cluster kubernetes-the-hard-way \ --certificate-authority=ca.crt \ --server=https://127.0.0.1:6443 \ --kubeconfig=admin.kubeconfig kubectl config set-credentials admin \ --client-certificate=admin.crt \ --embed-certs=true \ --kubeconfig=admin.kubeconfig kubectl config set-context default \ --cluster=kubernetes-the-hard-way \ --kubeconfig=admin.kubeconfig kubectl config use-context default \ --kubeconfig=admin.kubeconfig2.3 Distribute the Kubernetes Configuration FilesCopy the kubelet and kube-proxy kubeconfig files to the worker nodes:for host in node-0 node-1; do ssh root@$host "mkdir /var/lib/{kube-proxy,kubelet}" scp kube-proxy.kubeconfig \ root@$host:/var/lib/kube-proxy/kubeconfig \ root@$host:/var/lib/kubelet/kubeconfigCopy the controller kubeconfig files to the control-plane node:scp \ admin.kubeconfig \ kube-controller-manager.kubeconfig \ kube-scheduler.kubeconfig \3. Generating the Data Encryption Config and KeyBy default, Kubernetes does not encrypt secrets at rest. We’ll generate a random key and create an encryption-config.yaml so all sensitive data stored in etcd is protected with AES-CBC encryption.3.1 Generate an Encryption Keyexport ENCRYPTION_KEY=$(head -c 32 /dev/urandom | base64)3.2 Manually Create The Encryption Config File:Print and copy your generated encryption key:Create the encryption-config.yaml:vim encryption-config.yamlPaste in the following, your $ENCRYPTION_KEY where indicated:apiVersion: apiserver.config.k8s.io/v1kind: EncryptionConfiguration - resources: providers: keys: secret: ${ENCRYPTION_KEY} #manually copy and paste the encryption keyCopy the encryption-config.yaml encryption config file to the server container:scp encryption-config.yaml root@server:~/4. Bootstrapping the etcd ClusterKubernetes stores all of its cluster data in etcd. We’ll install and configure etcd on our control-plane node (server), secure it with TLS, and verify that it’s operational.On the jumpbox container, copy the etcd binaries and systemd unit file to the server node:scp \ downloads-amd/etcd-v3.4.34-linux-amd64.tar.gz \ root@server:~/Extract and install the etcd binaries:tar -xvf etcd-v3.4.34-linux-amd64.tar.gzmv etcd-v3.4.34-linux-amd64/etcd* /usr/local/bin/Create necessary directories:mkdir -p /etc/etcd /var/lib/etcdchmod 700 /var/lib/etcdCopy the required certificates into place:cp ca.crt kube-api-server.key kube-api-server.crt /etc/etcd/Move the systemd unit file into /etc/systemd/system:mv etcd.service /etc/systemd/system/4.3 Start the etcd Serversystemctl daemon-reloadsystemctl enable etcdCheck cluster membership:You should see something like this:6702b0a34e2cfd39, started, controller, http://127.0.0.1:2380, http://127.0.0.1:2379, falseView the status of etcd server. Look out for any errors and warnings: In Part 3, you have:Created a Certificate Authority and secured Kubernetes components with TLS certificates.Configured kubeconfig files to authenticate nodes, system components, and the admin user. for secrets at rest in etcd.Installed and bootstrapped etcd, providing Kubernetes with a reliable key-value store.With etcd online and secure communication in place, you’re ready to deploy the rest of the control plane and bring the worker nodes fully online. , where we’ll:Configure and launch the Kubernetes API server and controllers.Start containerd, kubelet, and kube-proxy on worker nodes.Perform a final smoke test to confirm everything is functioning as expected.

item.title: Constructing A Highly Available 2-Tier Architecture With Terraform
item.author: Trevor Boodram
item.content:encoded: Overview: Hello everyone and welcome to another documentation post today we will be going over in creating a 2-tier architecture with terraform.Terraform is an infrastructure as code tool which provides developers the ability to deploy resources more efficiently across many platforms.Terraform also enables us to reuse our code in case if we would need to create or add more of our resources to our infrastructure.Additionally RDS (Relational Database Service) gives another great benefit to cloud computing as it is setup to ensure high availability during across different availability zones,RDS performs frequent backups in times for safe data recovery.RDS can be deployed in a private VPC allowing for data encryption at rest and in transit.Then we will launch an EC2 Instance to have access to the web server in it’s public subnet.Firstly I started creating a repository and named my repository in this way we can upload our code easily with VS Code.Also created a Main.tf file and a Script file for our user data.Next I want to start off my script by associating the provider to AWS and we get the resource block from the terraform registry. This will allow the terraform environment to use it’s AWS service with the selected region.First I started off creating my VPC and set it’s default cidr and gave it a name.Next I started creating the public subnets and connected them to the new vpc that is created, also it creates an IP address ranges of the following subnets.I also added tags for easier identification.Then I also created Private subnets and associate it to my new vpc. Also created an IP address range of the following subnets and also provided names for easier identification.By creating private subnet it helps in keeping our resources private and the subnets that want to be public making it accessible from the internet.Next we move on by getting the resource block to create route tables and it’s routes our subnets to the vpc I got this from the terraform registry.We associate the routes to all Ipv4 traffic and it’s being directed to the internet gateway.Now we need to associate the route table and the subnets to the new VPC.This process will ensure that route tables can route there traffic with the proper subnets having proper ec2 instance communication.Next we can create the Internet Gateway this will allow our inbound and outbound internet traffic to reach the instances and to the web.Next we started creating the security groups from the terraform resource block I gave it a name and connected it to the vpc that we created earlier.We also want to associate the posts that we will need open and it allows HTTP,and SSH traffic.This carries out any IP Address traffic and send it to all outbound traffic.Next I also created a security group another resource security block which is for mySQL. We gave our security group a name connected the security group to the VPC.I allowed the necessary ports on my inbound creating all IP traffic and setting the egress(outbound). This carries out proper communication.# Creating EC2 Instance 2resource "aws_instance" "Ec2_instances2" { ami = "ami-xxxxxxxxxx" # Change It to your own AMI instance_type = "t2.micro" vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.web_sg.id] subnet_id = aws_subnet.public_subnet_2.id key_name = "Ec2Kp" user_data = base64encode(file("userdata_script.sh")) name = "Ec2_instance2"}We can start creating the Ec2 instances and I got the resource block from the terraform registry, we need to specify the right ami that we want to use and in this case I went with amazon Linux, next we define the instance type t2 micro. Also set the network configurations and connected them to the specified security group names.Then I also placed the subnets and provided a key pair.I already had a key pair so I will be reusing it.#!/bin/bashyum update -ysystemctl start httpd echo 'Hooray The Test Page Is Working :)' | sudo tee -a /var/www/html/index.htmlNext I provided a user data script and linked it to my instance so that anytime we launch the instances the script is executed. Then to end it off I added tags on our instance.Next we can start creating our Relational Database and it’s being connected to the VPC that we created.The RDS instance runs with MySQL it will identify the network configuration,security group, and the subent.Then we can create another resource block that specifies the private subnets to the rds database.This ensure that the subnets are private to the database .terraform initterraform validateterraform planterraform destroy We can now navigate back to the AWS Console to see that our following resources are now created like our VPC.Next we can see our subnets are created successfully and connected to the route tables and the main Internet Gateway.We can also see that the Internet Gateway was also created.We can also see that the security groups are also created.We can also see that the database was also created and is available.Next we can see that our ec2 instance is also created and running.Also the security groups is associated to the instance.Next we can copy the public IP from one of our running instances and paste it into another web browser and we are prompted with our running test page successfully.# Configure the AWS Providerterraform { aws = { source = "hashicorp/aws" version = "~> 5.0" } region = "us-east-1"# Create a VPC (Virtual Private Cloud)resource "aws_vpc" "Trevorvpc" { cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16" instance_tenancy = "default" Name = "Trevorvpc"}# Public Subnets for Web Server Tierresource "aws_subnet" "public_subnet_1" { vpc_id = aws_vpc.Trevorvpc.id cidr_block = "10.0.1.0/24" availability_zone = "us-east-1a" map_public_ip_on_launch = true Name = "PublicSubnet-1"}resource "aws_subnet" "public_subnet_2" { vpc_id = aws_vpc.Trevorvpc.id cidr_block = "10.0.2.0/24" availability_zone = "us-east-1b" map_public_ip_on_launch = true Name = "PublicSubnet-2"}# Private Subnets for RDS Tierresource "aws_subnet" "private_subnet_1" { vpc_id = aws_vpc.Trevorvpc.id cidr_block = "10.0.3.0/24" availability_zone = "us-east-1a" Name = "PrivateSubnet-1"}resource "aws_subnet" "private_subnet_2" { vpc_id = aws_vpc.Trevorvpc.id cidr_block = "10.0.4.0/24" availability_zone = "us-east-1b" Name = "PrivateSubnet-2"}resource "aws_route_table" "private_route_table" { vpc_id = aws_vpc.Trevorvpc.id}resource "aws_route" "private_route" { route_table_id = aws_route_table.private_route_table.id destination_cidr_block = "0.0.0.0/0" gateway_id = aws_internet_gateway.IGW.idresource "aws_route_table" "public_route_table" { vpc_id = aws_vpc.Trevorvpc.id}resource "aws_route" "public_route" { route_table_id = aws_route_table.public_route_table.id destination_cidr_block = "0.0.0.0/0" gateway_id = aws_internet_gateway.IGW.id# Public Route Table Associationresource "aws_route_table_association" "routetableassociation1" { subnet_id = aws_subnet.public_subnet_1.id route_table_id = aws_route_table.public_route_table.idresource "aws_route_table_association" "routetableassociation2" { subnet_id = aws_subnet.public_subnet_2.id route_table_id = aws_route_table.public_route_table.id}# Private Route Table Associationresource "aws_route_table_association" "privaterouteassociation1" { subnet_id = aws_subnet.private_subnet_1.id route_table_id = aws_route_table.private_route_table.idresource "aws_route_table_association" "privaterouteassociation2" { subnet_id = aws_subnet.private_subnet_2.id route_table_id = aws_route_table.private_route_table.id}# Create an Internet Gatewayresource "aws_internet_gateway" "IGW" { vpc_id = aws_vpc.Trevorvpc.id Name = "MainIGW"}# Creating a Security Group for Web Serverresource "aws_security_group" "web_sg" { description = "Allow inbound HTTP and SSH" vpc_id = aws_vpc.Trevorvpc.id from_port = 80 protocol = "tcp" cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"] } from_port = 22 protocol = "tcp" cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"] } from_port = 0 protocol = "-1" cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"] }# Security Group for RDS MySQLresource "aws_security_group" "rds_sg" { description = "Allow inbound MySQL" vpc_id = aws_vpc.Trevorvpc.id from_port = 3306 protocol = "tcp" cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"] } from_port = 0 protocol = "-1" cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"] }# Creating EC2 Instance 1 resource "aws_instance" "Ec2_instances1" { ami = "ami-085ad6ae776d8f09c" # Change It to your own AMI instance_type = "t2.micro" vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.web_sg.id] subnet_id = aws_subnet.public_subnet_1.id key_name = "Ec2Kp" user_data = base64encode(file("userdata_script.sh")) name = "Ec2_instance1"}# Creating EC2 Instance 2resource "aws_instance" "Ec2_instances2" { ami = "ami-085ad6ae776d8f09c" # Change It to your own AMI instance_type = "t2.micro" vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.web_sg.id] subnet_id = aws_subnet.public_subnet_2.id key_name = "Ec2Kp" user_data = base64encode(file("userdata_script.sh")) name = "Ec2_instance2"}resource "aws_db_instance" "mydb" { identifier = "mydb" engine = "mysql" engine_version = "5.7" instance_class = "db.t3.micro" allocated_storage = 20 db_name = "trevordatabase" username = "admin" password = "password" db_subnet_group_name = aws_db_subnet_group.dbmain.name vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.rds_sg.id] multi_az = false storage_type = "gp2" skip_final_snapshot = true}# Database for Subnet Groupresource "aws_db_subnet_group" "dbmain" { name = "main-subnet-group" subnet_ids = [aws_subnet.private_subnet_1.id, aws_subnet.private_subnet_2.id] Name = "MainDataBase"}Conclusion: Thank you for taking your time to read through my documentation. I’ve really enjoyed deploying the following resources with terraform making it easy and quicker for your resources to work you can defiantly scale the resources if you need to .

item.title: How HTTPS Works: A Deep Dive Into Secure Web Communication
item.author: Ahmed Adel
item.content:encoded: Imagine you’re sending a secret letter to your friend across the city. If you hand it to a random messenger without protection, anyone can read or even alter it before it reaches your friend. This is what happens when data is sent over the internet without encryption — it’s vulnerable to eavesdroppers and hackers.Now, what if you placed the letter in a locked box and only your friend had the key? No one else could read it. This is the essence of encryption — securing communication so only the intended recipient can access the information.But there’s still a problem: how do you safely share the key to unlock the box? If a spy intercepts it, they can read everything. To solve this, we use a special trick — two keys: one for locking and another for unlocking, ensuring the message stays safe. This is how HTTPS protects online communication, making sure your sensitive information — like passwords and credit card details — remains secure.Symmetric key algorithms use the same key for both encryption and decryption. Examples include AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) and DES (Data Encryption Standard).Asymmetric Key AlgorithmsAsymmetric key algorithms use a pair of keys: a public key for encryption and a private key for decryption. It is computationally infeasible to derive the private key from the public key. Examples include RSA (Rivest-Shamir-Adleman) and Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC).The public key is one of the key pairs used in asymmetric encryption. It can be freely shared with anyone.The private key is the other half of the asymmetric key pair. It must be kept secret, as it is used to decrypt messages encrypted with the corresponding public key.Hashing algorithms take any input and generate a fixed-length hash value. Hashes are one-way functions, meaning they cannot be reversed to obtain the original data. Examples include SHA-256, SHA-512, and MD5 (though MD5 is considered vulnerable to attacks).A digital signature is a cryptographic technique used to verify the integrity and authenticity of digital documents. The signing process involves:1. Hashing the document’s content using a hashing algorithm.2. Encrypting the hash using the document owner’s private key.3. Sending the document along with the encrypted hash.1. Decrypt the encrypted hash using the sender’s public key.2. Hash the received document and compare the two hashes.3. If the hashes match, the document is verified as unaltered and authentic.Certificate Authorities (CAs)Certificate Authorities (CAs) are trusted organizations responsible for issuing and signing digital certificates using their private key. Their public keys are pre-installed in operating systems and browsers to verify digital certificates.When a client and a server communicate over HTTPS, they must encrypt the transmitted data. There are two options for encryption:1. Symmetric encryption, which is fast but requires both parties to share a secret key.2. Asymmetric encryption, which solves the key-sharing issue but is slower.To balance security and performance, HTTPS uses asymmetric encryption only for securely exchanging the symmetric key. Once the symmetric key is securely shared, all further communication is encrypted using symmetric encryption.The Issue with Symmetric EncryptionIn symmetric encryption, both the client and server must share the same key for encryption and decryption. However, transmitting this key over an insecure network poses a major risk — attackers could intercept it and decrypt the data.How HTTPS Solves This ProblemHTTPS solves this issue by using asymmetric encryption to securely exchange the symmetric key. After that, symmetric encryption is used for all subsequent communication.Step-by-Step Example of HTTPS in Action1. SSL Certificate GenerationThe server owner generates an SSL/TLS certificate by providing its public key and organization details to a Certificate Authority (CA).The CA signs the certificate using its private key and issues it to the server owner.2. Client Requests the Server’s SSL CertificateWhen a client (e.g., a web browser) connects to an HTTPS server, it requests the server’s SSL certificate.The server responds by sending its SSL certificate, which contains its public key.3. Client Verifies the SSL CertificateThe client verifies the certificate by:Hashing the server’s public key.Decrypting the certificate’s digital signature using the CA’s public key (pre-installed in the OS/browser).Comparing the hashes. If they match, the certificate is valid and has not been tampered with.4. Securely Exchanging the Symmetric KeyThe client generates a pre-master secret (a temporary key used to derive the symmetric encryption key).The client encrypts the pre-master secret using the server’s public key and sends it to the server.The server decrypts the pre-master secret using its private key.Both the client and server use the pre-master secret to generate the symmetric session key.Now, both the client and server have a shared symmetric key.They use this key to encrypt and decrypt all subsequent communication.This ensures secure, high-speed data exchange between the client and server.HTTPS secures data transmission by combining asymmetric and symmetric encryption. It uses asymmetric encryption only for securely exchanging the symmetric key, and then switches to symmetric encryption for performance. This ensures that sensitive information remains private and tamper-proof during communication over the internet.
